component_layouts.layout_narrative: The child schedule button mirrors the adult CTA in placement and style but lives within the child new patient section. It appears beneath the combined child content area—after the main child copy and age guidance panel—so that parents read through guidance before reaching the appointment action. On desktop, with the two-column arrangement above, the button is centered under the entire 8–9 column span, visually tying both the text and age guidance panel together into a single flow. There is 24–32px of vertical space between the bottom of the last text or the age guidance panel (whichever ends lower) and the top of the button. The button uses the same brand color, size, and padding as the adult version (about 260–320px wide, with 12–16px vertical padding) to reinforce consistency. The Insurance link sits just beneath it, separated by 16–24px, serving as a secondary pathway if users want more financial info before booking.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline CTA within the child section that scrolls naturally with the content. Like the adult button, it is non-sticky and responsive, staying centered in the column and potentially widening modestly on smaller screens while maintaining comfortable side margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The insurance link is a subtle navigation element that appears as a small text-based link beneath the Schedule First Visit button in whichever section is active (adult or child). In layout terms, it is centered horizontally within the same content column as the button but visually lighter: no large filled background, likely just underlined text or a minimal outline treatment. It sits 16–24px below its corresponding schedule button, maintaining enough separation to clearly communicate its secondary nature while still being contextually connected. The link does not extend beyond the width of the text label itself, leaving generous whitespace on both sides within the column. On long pages, it appears near the bottom of the visible New Patients content, just above any subsequent global footer or additional site-wide sections, acting as a graceful step-down option for users not yet ready to book.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline navigation link that scrolls with the page. It appears within whichever section (adult or child) is active, and its vertical position shifts accordingly but it does not pin or float. On smaller screens, it remains centered and may wrap to multiple lines if the label is long, but it stays visually compact.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The guides loading state is a compact feedback indicator that appears inline within the child new patient section in place of—or directly above—the age guidance panel area while dynamic guidance content is being fetched. It occupies the same general horizontal region as the age guidance panel: on desktop, aligned to the right side of the central column where the panel would appear (about 30–35% width), and on mobile, full-width beneath the main child copy. Vertically, it is modest in height, typically 48–96px, with a centered spinner or shimmer skeleton and a short status message such as 'Loading age-specific guidance…' There is at least 16–24px of space above and below it to keep it from feeling cramped against the surrounding text or CTA. Once loading completes, this component collapses and is replaced by the fully rendered age guidance panel content without shifting the broader column structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline temporary placeholder within the child section. It scrolls with the page and appears only while guidance content is loading. Its space is directly reused by the age guidance panel once data is available, ensuring a stable layout without major jumps.

name: Special Offers
title: Special Offers
description: Displays current dental promotions with filtering by type, price, and new-patient eligibility, and links to promotion details or appointment booking.

core_features: Fetch and display a list of current promotions using the getSpecialOffersList interface, filtered by optional promotion type, maximum price, and new-patient eligibility.
Support URL-driven pre-filtering via promotionType, maxPrice, and isNewPatientOnly parameters so other pages (such as the homepage) can deep-link into a pre-filtered offers view.
Allow users to adjust filters on the page (promotion type, maximum price, new-patient-only) and update the URL parameters accordingly, then re-fetch the filtered promotions.
Visually highlight a specific promotion in the list when a highlightPromotionId URL parameter is provided, without changing the underlying dataset.
Provide navigation from each promotion to a promotion detail page (promotion_detail.html) with the relevant promotionId URL parameter.
Provide navigation from eligible promotions directly to the appointment booking page (appointment_booking.html), passing sourcePage, promotionId, and serviceId URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – View all current offers from navigation:
1. User clicks the “Special Offers” link in the header or footer, loading promotions.html with no query parameters.
2. The page state manager reads the empty query string and constructs a getSpecialOffersList call with onlyActive set so that only current promotions are returned and no additional filters are applied.
3. getSpecialOffersList returns an array of promotions.
4. The offers list component renders all returned promotions.
5. User can click a promotion title or a dedicated link to navigate to promotion_detail.html?promotionId=<promotion_id>.
6. If the promotion card includes a claim/schedule action, user can click it to navigate to appointment_booking.html with sourcePage=promotion_page, promotionId set from the selected promotion, and serviceId set from the promotion’s service_id.

Workflow 2 – Arrive on a pre-filtered whitening offers view from the homepage (including a maximum price constraint):
1. From index.html, the user selects a link or control that leads to promotions.html with promotionType, maxPrice, and isNewPatientOnly parameters in the URL (for example, promotionType, a numeric maxPrice, and isNewPatientOnly as 'true' or 'false').
2. The page state manager parses promotionType, maxPrice, and isNewPatientOnly from the URL and converts isNewPatientOnly to a boolean value where applicable.
3. The state manager calls getSpecialOffersList with promotionType, maxPrice, isNewPatientOnly, and onlyActive set so that only matching current promotions are returned.
4. The filter form component initializes its fields to reflect the URL parameters so the user sees which filters are applied.
5. The offers list component renders only the promotions returned by getSpecialOffersList, which may include whitening promotions priced at or below the maxPrice value.
6. The user can further refine or clear the filters; on submit, the page updates the URL query string and re-calls getSpecialOffersList to show updated results.

Workflow 3 – Highlight a specific promotion when deep-linked:
1. User clicks a deep link from index.html that navigates to promotions.html with highlightPromotionId in the URL, optionally combined with promotionType, maxPrice, and isNewPatientOnly.
2. The state manager parses all URL parameters and calls getSpecialOffersList with promotionType, maxPrice, isNewPatientOnly, and onlyActive.
3. After receiving results, the offers list component compares each promotion’s promotion_id against highlightPromotionId.
4. The matching promotion is rendered with a distinct highlighted state (for example, emphasized placement or styling), while all other promotions are rendered normally.
5. The user can click the highlighted promotion to view details or schedule an appointment, following the same navigation rules as other promotions.

Workflow 4 – Adjust filters directly on the Special Offers page:
1. User is viewing promotions.html with or without existing URL filters.
2. The user changes one or more fields in the filter form (e.g., selects a promotion type, enters or updates a maximum price, toggles new-patient-only eligibility).
3. On form submission, the filter component constructs a new query string based on the current form values (promotionType, maxPrice, isNewPatientOnly) and navigates to promotions.html with those parameters.
4. The state manager detects the URL change, parses the parameters, and calls getSpecialOffersList with the updated filter values and onlyActive.
5. The offers list re-renders with the new filtered results; the filter form remains in sync with the URL values so reloading or sharing the URL preserves the chosen filters.
6. The user may also reset the filters; on reset, the filter form clears its values, the page navigates to promotions.html without filtering parameters, and getSpecialOffersList is called without promotionType, maxPrice, or isNewPatientOnly.

Workflow 5 – Navigate from a promotion directly to booking:
1. User identifies a promotion of interest in the offers list.
2. The promotion card exposes a primary action (e.g., 'Claim Offer' or 'Schedule') when the promotion has a related service_id.
3. When the user clicks the primary action, the card’s event handler constructs a URL to appointment_booking.html with sourcePage set to 'promotion_page', promotionId set to the promotion’s promotion_id, and serviceId set to the promotion’s service_id.
4. The browser navigates to appointment_booking.html with these parameters.
5. The booking page can then initialize its own state based on the incoming promotionId and serviceId, creating an AppointmentRequest if appropriate (handled by that page’s interfaces).
interactions: On page load or when the URL changes, the state manager component reads promotionType, maxPrice, isNewPatientOnly, and highlightPromotionId from the query string, normalizes them, and triggers getSpecialOffersList with appropriate parameters.

When the filter form is submitted, the form’s submit handler serializes the current filter values into URL parameters (promotionType, maxPrice, isNewPatientOnly) and navigates to promotions.html with those query parameters, causing a refresh of the offers list via getSpecialOffersList.

When the user uses a reset or clear-filters control, the filter form removes the related query parameters from the URL and navigates to promotions.html without them, triggering a new getSpecialOffersList call with no filter parameters except onlyActive.
When a promotion title or a 'Learn More' action is clicked on a promotion card, the click handler navigates to promotion_detail.html?promotionId=<promotion_id> for the selected promotion.

When a 'Claim Offer' or 'Schedule' action is clicked on a promotion card with a defined service_id, the click handler navigates to appointment_booking.html with sourcePage=promotion_page, promotionId=<promotion_id>, and serviceId=<service_id>.
When getSpecialOffersList is in progress, a loading indicator component is displayed, and the offers list component is disabled or hidden until the response is received.

If getSpecialOffersList returns an empty array for the current filters, an empty-state component is shown explaining that no promotions match the selected criteria and offering an option to clear filters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL parameters as the single source of truth for filter and highlight state. On every page load and on every navigation to promotions.html, the state manager parses promotionType, maxPrice, isNewPatientOnly, and highlightPromotionId from the query string. If promotionType is present and non-empty, it is passed directly to getSpecialOffersList; if omitted or empty, the promotionType parameter is not supplied or is set to a null-equivalent in the interface call so that no type-based filtering occurs. If maxPrice is present and can be parsed as a finite number, that numeric value is passed to getSpecialOffersList as maxPrice; if not present or invalid, maxPrice is omitted in the call. If isNewPatientOnly is present, its string value is evaluated: if it equals 'true' (case-insensitive), the interface call receives a boolean true; if it equals 'false', the interface call receives a boolean false; otherwise, isNewPatientOnly is not supplied to the interface. The onlyActive parameter in getSpecialOffersList is always set so that only current promotions are returned. After the interface response is received, the offers list is bound to the returned array, and the highlightPromotionId (if present) is compared against each promotion’s promotion_id to determine which card should render in a highlighted state. The filter form fields are initialized from the current URL parameters so that the UI always reflects the actual filter state encoded in the URL. Any filter interaction that changes promotionType, maxPrice, or isNewPatientOnly results in a navigation that rewrites the query string, which in turn triggers a re-fetch via getSpecialOffersList. This URL-centric approach ensures that bookmarking, sharing, or reloading the page preserves the same filtered view and highlighted promotion.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, URL parameter parsing, and data fetching for the promotions list. It reads promotionType, maxPrice, isNewPatientOnly, and highlightPromotionId from the URL, prepares parameters for getSpecialOffersList, calls the interface, and exposes the resulting promotions array and highlightPromotionId to child components.

Provides controls for users to filter promotions by promotion type, maximum price, and new-patient-only eligibility. It synchronizes its field values with the URL parameters and triggers navigation to update the query string when filters are applied or cleared.

Displays the list of promotions returned by getSpecialOffersList. Receives the promotions array and highlightPromotionId from the state manager, determines which promotion (if any) should be highlighted, and renders a collection of offer cards.

Represents a single promotion within the offers list. Shows key information such as promotion name, promotion_type, price, original_price, is_new_patient_only status, eligibility_notes, and associated service_name. Provides actions to view more details or proceed directly to booking. Applies a highlighted state when its promotion_id matches the highlightPromotionId provided by the parent.

Displays a message when getSpecialOffersList returns no promotions for the current filter criteria. Offers the user options such as clearing filters or adjusting criteria, which modify the URL parameters and trigger a new data fetch.
Indicates that promotions are being loaded from getSpecialOffersList. It becomes visible when a fetch is in progress and hides when the fetch completes or fails.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a max-width content container (~1140–1200px). The row of four circular service cards suggests four equal-width columns on desktop.
layout.layout_pattern: The page continues the global layout established by the mockup: the existing header, hero imagery, and circular service cards occupy the top 40–50% of the initial viewport. The Special Offers content then lives in the central main column beneath this band. Within this column, the hierarchy flows top-to-bottom: page title and intro (handled by the page template), then the Special Offers filter form as a horizontal control band, followed by the list of offers (or its empty/loading states). The offer cards are arranged in a simple grid within this central column, reading left-to-right, top-to-bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page continues the global layout established by the mockup: the existing header, hero imagery, and circular service cards occupy the top 40–50% of the initial viewport. The Special Offers content then lives in the central main column beneath this band. Within this column, the hierarchy flows top-to-bottom: page title and intro (handled by the page template), then the Special Offers filter form as a horizontal control band, followed by the list of offers (or its empty/loading states). The offer cards are arranged in a simple grid within this central column, reading left-to-right, top-to-bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column sits centered on the page at about 60–70% of the viewport width (max ~1140–1200px container, with the offers content constrained to ~70–80% of that). Vertically, the header/hero/circular cards occupy the upper half of the first screen; the Special Offers section takes the lower half and continues below the fold as needed. Inside the offers section, the H1 + intro block occupies roughly 20% of the section height, the filter form around 15–20%, and the offers list takes the remaining space. Vertical spacing between H1, filter form, and offers list follows the 32–48px rhythm from the mockup to keep elements clearly separated but cohesive.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density mirrors the mockup’s moderately spacious, editorial feel. The filter form is compact but not cramped, with plenty of breathing room between fields. Offer cards are generous in padding (around 20–24px internal padding) and spaced apart by 24–32px both vertically and horizontally, avoiding a cramped grid. There are clear visual pauses between the H1/introduction, the filter band, and the grid of offers, avoiding the feel of a dense product catalog and instead conveying a curated set of promotions.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Within the existing global Z-pattern, the eye moves from the top hero area down to the centered Special Offers heading, then horizontally across the filter controls (left-to-right over type, price, and eligibility filter), and then down into the offers list where cards are scanned left-to-right within each row, then down to subsequent rows. The filter form acts as a secondary horizontal bar echoing the navigation bar above, while the offer cards form a strong vertical rhythm, leading the user downward through the available promotions.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing header and circular service cards, the Special Offers page content begins in a centered main column that feels like a continuation of the mockup’s editorial area. At the top of this column, a large, center-aligned “Special Offers” heading and a short, friendly subheading introduce the section, with generous 32–40px breathing room above and below. Directly beneath, a soft, card-like filter band spans about 70–80% of the content width, centered, with horizontally arranged dropdowns and toggles that echo the tidy structure of the main navigation. Below this filter band, the page opens into a grid of promotion cards within the same central column: on desktop, two cards per row, each with rounded corners, light shadows, and strong teal accents that tie back to the site’s brand. The cards are separated by comfortable gaps, creating a rhythm of distinct yet related offers. If no promotions match the filters, a centered empty-state message appears in this same grid area; while offers are loading, a subtle overlay with a spinner sits over the grid. Throughout, the layout feels trustworthy and calm, with clear visual hierarchy and a familiar, slightly traditional medical aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component functions as a non-visual controller and does not present any visible UI. It sits logically at the top of the Special Offers section in the DOM, wrapping the filter form and offers list, but it occupies no explicit space on the page. In terms of layout, it behaves like an invisible container that governs which child content is shown (loading indicator, list, or empty state) without altering the spatial arrangement defined by the visible components. It does not introduce padding or margins; the visible vertical spacing is entirely determined by the filter form and list containers.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible structural wrapper; participates in document flow but renders no visual box and does not affect layout spacing directly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter form appears directly beneath the page’s Special Offers H1 and intro text, centered within the main content column. It spans roughly 70–80% of the inner content width (within the ~1140–1200px max container), leaving generous side margins so it feels like a contained band rather than edge-to-edge controls. There is approximately 32–40px vertical spacing between the intro text and the top of the filter form, and about 32–40px between the bottom of the form and the start of the offers list. Visually, the form sits inside a low-elevation card: a light background (e.g., soft gray or very pale teal), 16–24px internal padding, slightly rounded corners, and a subtle drop shadow that harmonizes with the circular cards in the hero area. On desktop, the controls are arranged in a single horizontal row aligned with the grid system: the offer type dropdown occupies the left third to 40% of the form width, the price range filter sits in the middle third, and the new-patient eligibility toggle or checkbox block is right-aligned in the remaining width. Beneath or to the far right of these, an inline text link for “Clear filters” sits to the left, while a primary teal “Apply” or “Update results” button anchors the right side, reinforcing the left-to-right scanning pattern. On narrower screens, these fields stack vertically with 12–16px vertical gaps, but remain centered within the same card, maintaining the spacious, friendly feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the page as part of the normal document flow. It anchors the top of the Special Offers functional area but does not stick to the viewport; it moves off-screen as the user scrolls down through a long list of offers.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The offers list container sits immediately below the filter form, sharing the same central alignment and width. Its left and right edges line up with the filter form’s card edges, creating a clean vertical column. There is a 32–48px vertical gap between the bottom of the filter form and the first row of offers, acting as a visual separator between controls and results. Within this container, offers are displayed in a grid with two cards per row on standard desktop widths: each card occupies approximately 48% of the list container’s width, leaving a 4%–6% horizontal gap between them and slightly larger margins at the container’s sides. Vertically, rows are spaced by 24–32px, creating a clear but not excessive separation between rows of promotions. The overall list can extend well below the fold; as the user scrolls, this grid simply continues downward within the main column, maintaining consistent left and right alignment with the header content above. The list also serves as the visual frame for both the empty state and loading indicator: when there are no offers, the empty state is vertically centered in a block of at least 40vh in height within this same width; when loading, a semi-transparent overlay sits inside this container without affecting the layout above or below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flexible-height scrollable area within the main page flow. It expands vertically to fit the number of offers, with no internal scrolling; the page’s main scroll bar controls movement through the list. It also acts as the positioning context for the loading overlay.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each special offer card is a rectangular, softly rounded panel that sits within the grid defined by the offers list. On desktop, a card occupies roughly half the list width (about 48%), with 24px of internal padding on all sides to match the spacious, marketing-style feel. Cards use a light background (white or very pale tint), a subtle drop shadow, and 8–12px corner radius, echoing the circular feature cards from the mockup but in a more content-friendly rectangular form. Inside each card, the structure follows a left-to-right emphasis pattern: along the top, a small, circular or pill-shaped badge (e.g., “New Patient Special” or a percentage off) sits near the top-left, using a gradient or solid brand teal/green fill to attract attention. To the right of this badge, aligned to the top-right corner, the price or discount highlight is displayed in a slightly larger sans-serif type, possibly with a contrasting color or weight. Below this top row, the offer title appears as a serif or semi-serif heading (e.g., H3), left-aligned and spanning roughly 70–80% of the card width, with a 8–12px gap beneath the badge/pricing line. Under the title, a brief description and key conditions are laid out in smaller sans-serif body text, with 4–8px of line spacing and a total text block height of about 2–4 lines to keep the card from feeling text-heavy. At the bottom of the card, a horizontal action area contains the primary CTA button (e.g., “View details” or “Book now”) aligned to the right, with 16px vertical padding in the button and sufficient horizontal padding to feel tappable (16–20px). To the left of the button, supporting text such as “New patients only” or “Expires [date]” appears in a subdued color. The card height is flexible but typically around 200–260px for an average offer, ensuring a consistent, tidy grid. On smaller screens, each card becomes full-width within the list, stacking one per row while maintaining the same internal structure and spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard responsive cards that resize and reflow based on available width. They participate in the list’s grid and have no independent scrolling or sticky behavior. They may use a subtle hover elevation or shadow intensification on desktop, but remain within their allocated grid cells.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state message appears inside the same central area used by the offers list, replacing the grid of cards when no promotions match the current filters. It is horizontally centered within the list container and vertically positioned so that the core content (icon and message) sits roughly in the upper-middle of the viewport, with at least 40vh of height reserved to avoid feeling cramped. A simple, friendly icon (e.g., an outlined tooth or magnifying glass) sits at the top of this block, centered, followed by a concise headline explaining that no offers were found, then a single line or two of guidance text in smaller body type suggesting adjusting filters or showing all offers. A secondary action link or ghost button such as “Reset filters” is centered beneath this text, approximately 16–24px below the copy. The empty state inherits the same width constraints as the list (about 70–80% of the content width) and maintains 24–32px spacing from the filter form above, maintaining the visual rhythm of the page. The surrounding background remains the same as the page background, so the empty state feels integrated with the section rather than like a separate modal.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears conditionally within the offers list container, replacing the grid of cards. It scrolls with the page but, due to its compact height, will usually be fully visible without requiring additional scrolling when filters change.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator overlays the special-offers-list area whenever promotions are being fetched or filters are updated. It does not shift surrounding content; instead, it appears as a semi-transparent veil over the existing list container. The overlay matches the exact width and horizontal position of the list, stretching from the top edge of the list (immediately under the filter form) to at least the height of the currently visible list content, with a 40–60% white or light background opacity to gently dim the offer cards below. At the geometric center of this overlay (horizontally centered, vertically centered relative to the visible list area), a simple, brand-colored spinner or progress indicator is displayed, possibly accompanied by a small, muted “Loading offers…” label beneath it. The filter form above remains fully visible and not covered, reinforcing that the system is responding to their filter changes specifically within the results area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned absolutely within the bounds of the special-offers-list container, effectively fixed relative to that container while it is in view. It does not affect layout flow or scroll behavior and is removed once loading completes, revealing the updated offers list or empty state underneath.

name: Promotion Detail
title: Promotion Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a single promotion and routes users into the appointment booking flow with the promotion and related service pre-selected.

core_features: Read the promotionId from the URL and load the corresponding promotion using getPromotionDetail.
Display detailed promotion information including name, description, price, original price (if provided), promotion type, active/expired status, and eligibility notes.

Display related service information (name, category, subtype, description, uninsured price, price notes) using the related_service returned by getPromotionDetail (and optionally refreshed via getServiceDetails).
Clearly indicate whether the promotion is currently redeemable based on is_currently_redeemable and status.
Highlight eligibility requirements such as whether the promotion is new-patient only (using is_new_patient_only and promotion.is_new_patient_only) and any terms_and_conditions.

Provide a primary action button (e.g., Claim Offer / Schedule Whitening) that, when enabled, navigates to appointment_booking.html with sourcePage, promotionId, serviceId, ageGroup, and isNewPatientPromotion URL parameters.
Provide a secondary navigation link back to the full promotions list (promotions.html).
Handle error or edge states when promotionId is missing, invalid, or the promotion is not active or not currently redeemable.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: View promotion from promotions list
1. User clicks a promotion on promotions.html.
2. Browser navigates to promotion_detail.html with ?promotionId=<selectedPromotionId>.
3. On load, the page controller reads promotionId from the URL.
4. The page calls getPromotionDetail(promotionId).
5. When data is returned, the promotion information and related_service are stored in page state.
6. The promotion detail sections render: main promotion summary, pricing, eligibility, and terms.
7. If is_currently_redeemable is true, the Claim Offer / Schedule button is enabled; otherwise, it is disabled or replaced with non-interactive messaging.
8. User may click Back to all offers to return to promotions.html, or click the primary action button to proceed to booking.

Workflow 2: View promotion from homepage
1. User clicks a featured promotion on index.html.
2. Browser navigates to promotion_detail.html with ?promotionId=<featuredPromotionId>.
3. The same loading flow as in Workflow 1 occurs via getPromotionDetail.
4. User reviews promotion details.
5. User clicks Claim Offer / Schedule button.
6. Browser navigates to appointment_booking.html with URL parameters: sourcePage=promotion_page, promotionId=<currentPromotionId>, serviceId=<relatedServiceId>, ageGroup=adult, isNewPatientPromotion=true.
7. The booking page uses these parameters to pre-select the service and apply the promotion context.

Workflow 3: User inspects more detailed service information
1. After promotion details load, the user chooses to view more details about the underlying service (e.g., procedure description and pricing context).
2. The page either uses the related_service returned from getPromotionDetail or, if a deeper refresh is desired, calls getServiceDetails(serviceId) where serviceId is promotion.service_id.
3. Detailed service fields (such as description, category, uninsured_price, price_notes) are displayed in a dedicated section or expandable area.
4. User can then proceed by clicking the Claim Offer / Schedule button, navigating to appointment_booking.html with the promotion and service pre-selected.

Workflow 4: Handling invalid or unavailable promotions
1. User navigates to promotion_detail.html without a promotionId, or with a promotionId that does not exist or is not retrievable.
2. The page attempts to read promotionId from the URL; if missing or invalid, it sets an error state without calling getPromotionDetail, or handles an error returned from getPromotionDetail.
3. The page displays an error state and provides a link to promotions.html for the user to browse other offers.
4. If the promotion is loaded but is_currently_redeemable is false or status indicates it is not active, the page clearly indicates that the offer cannot be claimed and disables or hides the Claim Offer / Schedule button, while still showing the core information and a link back to promotions.html.
interactions: On page load: The page controller reads the promotionId URL parameter and triggers getPromotionDetail(promotionId).
On promotion data load success: The page updates its internal state with promotion, related_service, is_currently_redeemable, and is_new_patient_only, then renders all dependent components.
On promotion data load error: The page enters an error state and shows a generic error message with a link to promotions.html.
On click Back to all offers: Navigate to promotions.html without additional parameters.

On click View service details (if provided): Use related_service from getPromotionDetail or call getServiceDetails(serviceId) with the promotion.service_id to display or refresh detailed service information.

On click Claim Offer / Schedule Whitening (when enabled): Navigate to appointment_booking.html with URL parameters sourcePage=promotion_page, promotionId set to the current promotion.id, serviceId set to the related_service.id (or promotion.service_id), ageGroup=adult, and isNewPatientPromotion=true.

On URL change (e.g., user navigates directly to another promotionId via link or browser history): The page controller detects the new promotionId, resets state to loading, and repeats the data-fetching process.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the promotionId URL parameter. On initialization and whenever the URL changes, the page reads promotionId from the query string (e.g., promotion_detail.html?promotionId=<id>). If promotionId is present, getPromotionDetail(promotionId) is called. The response populates the core page state: promotion (Promotion entity), related_service (Service entity), is_currently_redeemable (boolean), and is_new_patient_only (boolean). These values determine which components render and whether actions are enabled. If promotionId is missing or invalid, or if getPromotionDetail fails, the page enters an error state and provides navigation back to promotions.html. A derived state flag (e.g., hasLoadedSuccessfully, isLoading, hasError) controls whether loading indicators, content, or error messaging is displayed. When the user clicks the Claim Offer / Schedule button, navigation to appointment_booking.html is performed using URL parameters assembled from the current state: sourcePage is set to promotion_page; promotionId is set to promotion.id; serviceId is set to related_service.id; ageGroup is set to adult; isNewPatientPromotion is set to true. No localStorage is used; all behavior is driven by the URL parameters and in-memory page state that is re-created on each load.

components.functionality: Coordinates reading the URL parameter, calling getPromotionDetail, handling loading and error states, and providing loaded data to child components. Also assembles URL parameters for navigation to the booking page.

Reads promotionId from the query string and calls getPromotionDetail(promotionId). Stores the result (promotion, related_service, is_currently_redeemable, is_new_patient_only) in the page state and exposes it to presentation components.

Optionally fetches up-to-date service information for the promotion's service by calling getServiceDetails(serviceId) when a user requests more detailed service information or when the controller determines a refresh is needed. Overrides or supplements the related_service data returned by getPromotionDetail.

Displays key promotion details such as promotion name, promotion_type, basic description, and high-level indication of whether it is currently redeemable. Uses is_currently_redeemable and promotion.status to show current availability state.

Displays pricing information specific to the promotion, using promotion.price and promotion.original_price if available. May show comparison between promotion price and the related service's uninsured_price, without hardcoding any numeric values.

Explains who qualifies for the promotion using promotion.is_new_patient_only, page-level is_new_patient_only, and promotion.eligibility_notes. Presents a clear indication if the offer is restricted to new patients.

Displays the promotion.terms_and_conditions field and any additional eligibility or usage notes from promotion.eligibility_notes. This component ensures users can see all conditions before claiming the offer.

Displays summarized information about the related dental service, such as service.name, category, service_subtype, and a short description. Provides context for what the promotion includes clinically.

Allows the user to expand or request more detailed service information. When activated, it triggers service-detail-refresher to call getServiceDetails(serviceId) if a refresh is needed, then displays additional service fields such as description, uninsured_price, and price_notes.

Provides the primary action button used to claim the promotion or schedule the related service. Uses is_currently_redeemable and promotion.status to determine if the button is enabled. When clicked, constructs the navigation URL to appointment_booking.html using values from the current promotion and related_service.
Provides a simple navigation control that sends the user back to the full promotions list at promotions.html. Does not depend on any API data.

Displays the current status of the promotion (e.g., active, expired, upcoming) based on promotion.status and is_currently_redeemable. May show a non-interactive message when the offer is not redeemable, informing the user while leaving the details visible.
Handles cases where promotionId is missing, invalid, or getPromotionDetail fails. Shows a generic error state and provides a link using back-to-offers-link to navigate to promotions.html.
Displays a loading state while getPromotionDetail (and optionally getServiceDetails) are in progress. Hides itself when data load completes or errors out.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a max-width content container (~1140–1200px). The row of four circular service cards suggests four equal-width columns on desktop.
layout.layout_pattern: The page content sits within the existing site shell (hero band, logo, nav, circular service cards). All promotion components live in a centered main column that begins directly beneath the row of circular cards. The arrangement is linear and top-to-bottom: a small back-navigation link at the top-left of this column, followed by a prominent promotion summary card that holds the title, key value props, status, and the main claim-offer CTA. Below this summary, detail sections (pricing, eligibility, service information, and terms) are stacked vertically with generous separation, mirroring the editorial/marketing style from the mockup. Non-visual controllers and loaders are embedded logically at the top of this content column and around the service summary, without claiming visible layout space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page content sits within the existing site shell (hero band, logo, nav, circular service cards). All promotion components live in a centered main column that begins directly beneath the row of circular cards. The arrangement is linear and top-to-bottom: a small back-navigation link at the top-left of this column, followed by a prominent promotion summary card that holds the title, key value props, status, and the main claim-offer CTA. Below this summary, detail sections (pricing, eligibility, service information, and terms) are stacked vertically with generous separation, mirroring the editorial/marketing style from the mockup. Non-visual controllers and loaders are embedded logically at the top of this content column and around the service summary, without claiming visible layout space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header, hero imagery, and circular cards still occupy roughly the top 40–50% of the initial viewport. The promotion detail content starts just below and uses a max-width container of about 1140–1200px, centered on the page. Within that container, the main text column for this page’s components occupies roughly 60–70% of the width, centered, leaving comfortable side gutters. The promotion summary section takes up most of the upper half of the first viewable portion of the promotion content, ensuring the title, key info, status, and claim CTA are visible without scrolling. The subsequent detail sections share the same column width and spacing rhythm, each separated vertically by around 32–48px to maintain the airy, approachable feel of the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a moderately spacious density. The promotion summary presents concise, high-impact information with a large title, a short descriptive paragraph, a few key bullets or highlights, and a clearly separated CTA group. Detail sections below use clear headings with short paragraphs and lists rather than long, dense blocks, with noticeable vertical gaps between headings and body text. Overall, the page feels marketing/editorial—easy to skim, with clear chunks of information and plenty of white space—rather than data-heavy or cramped.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows the mockup’s Z-pattern adapted to this detail page. After the global header, the eye lands at the top-left of the promotion column on the 'Back to offers' link, then moves horizontally across the top of the promotion summary where the promotion status and the primary 'Claim offer / Book now' CTA group sit towards the right. From there the gaze drops into the center of the promotion summary card (title and short description), then continues downward into the stacked detail sections in a straight, linear scroll. The strong, centered summary block acts as a visual anchor, and the repeated use of aligned section headings and consistent column width guides users smoothly down through pricing, eligibility, service details, and finally the terms.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing site header, hero, and row of circular service cards, a centered promotion detail column begins. It sits within a max-width container, with generous gutters on both sides so the content feels comfortably framed on large screens. At the very top of this column, a subtle 'Back to offers' link appears on the left, giving users an immediate escape route. Just beneath it, a prominent white promotion summary card with a soft drop shadow spans about two-thirds of the page width, centered and slightly elevated off the background. The top edge of this card holds the promotion title on the left and a small colored status pill and the main claim-offer buttons on the right, creating the familiar left-to-right Z eye movement. Below this header row, concise copy explains the promotion, possibly accompanied by one or two key value callouts. The main CTA group is clearly visible within this card, aligned toward the right on desktop but still comfortably within the card’s boundaries. As users scroll down, the card gives way to a series of cleanly separated sections—Pricing, Eligibility, Service Details (with a toggle for more information), and Terms—each anchored with a clear heading and light content, all aligned within the same central column. The page feels orderly and trustworthy, with traditional typography, teal accents, and a structured vertical rhythm that makes it easy to find the key facts before committing to booking through the promotion CTA.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the main promotion content area as a centered column directly beneath the global hero and circular card row. It constrains the content to a max width of about 1140–1200px, with the primary readable column occupying roughly 60–70% of that width and centered horizontally. It provides generous top padding of around 32–48px from the bottom of the circular cards to the first promotion element, and it enforces consistent vertical spacing (around 32px) between all child sections (summary, pricing, eligibility, service summary, terms). The controller itself is not visually represented but establishes this central, linear flow and spacing grid for all promotion-related components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a static, scrollable container for all promotion components within the main page content area, adhering to the 12-column grid and central alignment but not fixed or floating.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promotion loader sits logically at the top of the promotion-detail-page-controller, just inside the boundaries of the main content column. It does not occupy permanent visual space; instead, when active, it temporarily replaces the promotion content area beneath the header with a loading state. Its effective region matches the promotion content column (about 60–70% of the page width, centered), ensuring any indication of loading appears in the same visual zone where the promotion summary and details would normally be.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visible when inactive; when active, it triggers the promotion-loading-indicator to overlay or occupy the central promotion column while preventing other promotion components from rendering.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-detail-refresher is conceptually positioned alongside the service-summary-section within the same central column, but it does not produce its own visible UI. It is associated with the region below the service summary heading, refreshing the underlying service details when the toggle is used or relevant data changes, without altering the spatial arrangement of the existing content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible functional element that runs within the bounds of the service-summary-section, without reserving dedicated layout space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promotion summary section is the first substantive visual block within the promotion column and sits about 24–32px below the 'Back to offers' link. It appears as a centered, card-like panel spanning roughly 65–70% of the viewport width within the max-width container, with equal left and right margins. The card uses ample internal padding (around 24–32px on all sides) and a subtle drop shadow consistent with the circular cards from the hero band. Within this card, the top row aligns the promotion title and key identifier on the left, while the promotion-status-indicator and promotion-claim-offer-cta group align to the right, mimicking the mockup’s header-to-CTA Z-pattern. Below this top row, a short, legible body block explains the promotion in 1–3 brief paragraphs or a paragraph plus a small bullet list, centered within the card’s width but left-aligned for readability. The card occupies roughly the upper third of the promotion content area in the initial viewport, ensuring that users see the title, status, and main CTA without scrolling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card element that scrolls with the page, visually anchoring the promotion detail content at the top of the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promotion pricing section is positioned directly below the promotion summary card, with a vertical gap of about 32–40px to preserve the airy feel. It shares the same central column width (about 60–70% of the viewport width within the container) and is horizontally centered, but it appears as a simpler text block rather than a heavy card—typically a heading followed by key pricing details. The heading (e.g., 'Pricing & Savings') aligns with the left edge of the textual content, and beneath it, pricing information is presented in short lines or a tidy list, allowing quick scanning. Overall, the section’s height is modest, occupying roughly a quarter of the viewport height on average screens before scrolling is needed further down.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content block that follows the established vertical rhythm, with no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promotion eligibility section appears directly beneath the pricing section, separated by roughly 32px of vertical space. It maintains the same central column width and alignment, creating a continuous vertical spine of information. The eligibility heading sits at the top of this block, with content such as bullet-point criteria or short paragraphs arranged in a vertically spaced list. It likely occupies a similar vertical footprint to the pricing section or slightly more if the criteria list is longer, but still keeps the content compact and approachable rather than overwhelming.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that scrolls naturally, visually grouped with the other detail sections by consistent spacing and alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promotion terms section is placed toward the lower part of the promotion detail stack, beneath the service-summary-section with about 32px of vertical gap separating them. It shares the same centered column width and left-aligned text. Its heading (e.g., 'Terms & Conditions') anchors the top of the section, with the legal or fine-print copy below, likely in slightly smaller type than the main body to reflect its secondary importance. The content is broken into short paragraphs or concise bullet points to avoid visual heaviness. This section may extend more vertically than others but is still buffered above and below with generous whitespace to maintain the open, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static lower-page content block, forming the tail end of the promotion detail stack and scrolling offscreen as the user moves past it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service summary section is positioned between the promotion-eligibility-section and the promotion-terms-section, separated from each by around 32px of vertical spacing. It continues the same central column width (about 60–70% of the container) and is centered horizontally. The top of this section features a heading such as the associated service name, aligned left. Just to the right or inline with this heading, the service-detail-toggle appears as a subtle control (for example, a link or chevron label like 'View full service details'). Below the heading and toggle, a brief overview of the dental service is shown—1–2 short paragraphs or a small bullet list—giving enough context to reassure users what treatment the promotion applies to without overwhelming them. When expanded via the toggle, any additional detail content extends downward within this same section, increasing its vertical height but remaining constrained to the same column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section whose height can dynamically expand or collapse based on the service-detail-toggle, but which remains anchored in its position between eligibility and terms.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service detail toggle is placed at the top of the service-summary-section, visually connected to the service heading. On desktop, it sits to the right of the service title on the same horizontal line, aligned with the right side of the text column, creating a subtle right-hand focal point in the section header. On narrower screens, it may wrap to a line directly beneath the heading but still right-aligned relative to the text column. The control itself is small and unobtrusive, using understated text and/or an icon, ensuring it doesn’t compete with the main promotion CTA but is easy to spot for users interested in more information.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the service-summary heading row; toggles the expansion or collapse of additional content inside the service-summary-section without changing its horizontal position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promotion claim-offer CTA group resides within the promotion-summary-section, positioned in the upper right portion of the summary card to complete the Z-pattern from title to action. On desktop, it typically appears as one prominent primary button (e.g., 'Claim Offer' or 'Book with This Offer') accompanied by an optional secondary link or button (e.g., 'Call Now'), aligned to the right edge of the card’s internal content area. Vertically, the CTA group is aligned roughly with the baseline of the promotion title or sits just below it, ensuring it appears above the center of the card and is visible without scrolling. On narrower screens, the CTA group shifts to span the full width of the card, stacking buttons vertically with 8–16px separation and aligning them centrally, while preserving comfortable top and bottom padding. Its size and color treatment make it one of the most visually prominent elements in the summary card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the summary card but positioned high enough to be visible on initial load; on mobile, it may adopt a near-full-width button treatment and can optionally become sticky at the bottom edge of the viewport once the user scrolls past the summary to keep the key action readily available.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-offers link is the first element inside the promotion-detail-page-controller’s content column, sitting flush with the left edge of the text column and about 32px below the bottom of the circular card band from the hero. It is a small, lightweight text link, optionally with a left-pointing arrow icon, and occupies a single line. There is around 8–16px of vertical space between this link and the top edge of the promotion-summary-section card. Its subtlety keeps it from competing with the main content, but its top-left placement aligns with users’ expectations for a back or breadcrumb control in this style of layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline navigation element that scrolls off the top of the page as the user moves down through the promotion details.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promotion status indicator appears within the top portion of the promotion-summary-section card, opposite the main title. It is styled as a compact pill or badge (for example, 'Limited Time' or 'Active until June 30'), aligned to the right side of the card’s content area, typically above or to the left of the promotion-claim-offer-cta group so that both elements form a cohesive right-aligned cluster. Its horizontal position aligns with the right edge of the text column; vertically, it sits near the top of the card, creating a clear visual association with the promotion’s current availability. The badge size is small relative to the CTA button but still distinct enough, with a brand-colored background and subtle shadow, to be noticed at a glance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored inside the promotion summary header row, moving with the card as the page scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promotion error state replaces the normal promotion content when an error occurs. It occupies the same central column as the other promotion sections (about 60–70% of the viewport width, centered), and appears in the vertical space where the promotion-summary-section would typically sit. The error UI is usually a card or panel with a soft shadow and brand-consistent styling, centered horizontally with generous padding (around 24–32px) and extra vertical margin from the hero band (about 48px) to clearly separate it. The error message text is centered or gently left-aligned, with any retry or navigation actions placed beneath it. When this state is shown, none of the other promotion sections (summary, pricing, eligibility, service, terms) are rendered below, ensuring the user’s attention is focused on the problem and the next available actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the entire promotion detail stack within the promotion-detail-page-controller; static within the scroll but centered high in the viewport so it’s visible immediately.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promotion loading indicator appears when data is being fetched, overlaying or occupying the central promotion column area. Visually, it is centered both horizontally and vertically within the available viewport area below the header and circular card band, so users immediately see that the promotion content is loading where they expect the summary to be. The background of the main content column may be lightly dimmed or left neutral, with the loader (spinner or progress indicator) and a short loading message occupying a compact area roughly in the middle of the screen. No other promotion content is displayed beneath it while active.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or replaces the promotion content region within the promotion-detail-page-controller; fixed relative to the viewport’s main content area while loading, then removed once data is ready so the standard stacked layout can appear.

name: Contact & Cost Estimates
title: Contact & Cost Estimates
description: Page where patients can send questions and request treatment cost estimates, including uninsured scenarios, using a single configurable contact form.

core_features: Load dynamic contact form configuration (submission types, preferred contact methods, maximum message length) via getContactFormConfig.
Render a unified contact form that adapts to the selected submission type (e.g., general questions vs. cost estimates) using configuration from getContactFormConfig.
Initialize the form from incoming URL parameters (submissionType, subject, relatedServiceIds, isUninsured, messageTemplate) to support deep-linking from other pages.

Support uninsured cost estimate requests by allowing users to indicate uninsured status, reference specific services by ID, and type a total estimated cost into the message and a dedicated numeric field.
Validate required fields (contact details, message, submission type) and enforce the maximum message length defined in the configuration.
Submit the completed form to the backend via submitContactSubmission and map the response (success flag, status, confirmation message, submission ID) into page state.
Display a confirmation panel after successful submission, including the backend-provided confirmation message and a link back to the homepage (index.html).
Allow users to correct validation or submission errors and resubmit without losing previously entered form data during the current page session.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Submit a general question from navigation
1. User clicks the “Contact” link in the header or “Contact & Cost Estimates” in the footer, navigating to contact.html with no URL parameters.
2. On page load, the page calls getContactFormConfig to retrieve submission types, preferred contact methods, and max message length.
3. The form initializes using the configuration; if a default submission type is determined by the UI logic (e.g., the first provided type), it is preselected.
4. The user optionally edits the submission type (e.g., to a general inquiry type), enters a subject and message, and provides their name, email, optional phone, and preferred contact method.
5. The user submits the form. The page validates required fields and message length against max_message_length.
6. If validation passes, the page calls submitContactSubmission with the current form values.
7. If the API returns success, the page shows the confirmation panel with the returned message and status, hides or disables the form, and offers a link to return to index.html.
8. If the API indicates failure, an error banner is shown and the user can correct input or retry submission.

Workflow 2: Request an uninsured cost estimate for a multi-step treatment from the Services & Pricing page
1. On services_pricing.html, the user chooses to request a cost estimate for one or more treatments and indicates that they are uninsured. That page navigates to contact.html with URL parameters such as submissionType, subject, relatedServiceIds, isUninsured, and messageTemplate.
2. On page load, contact.html calls getContactFormConfig, then parses the URL parameters.
3. The form preselects the submission type using the submissionType URL parameter if it matches a configured submission type value; otherwise, the UI falls back to its default selection logic.
4. The subject field is prefilled from the subject URL parameter if present. The message field is prefilled from messageTemplate if provided; the user can edit this text.
5. The form parses relatedServiceIds into an internal array and stores it as part of the form state. These IDs are not resolved on this page but are included in the final submission.
6. The isUninsured parameter is parsed into a boolean and used to set the initial uninsured toggle or checkbox.
7. Because the selected submission type corresponds to a cost estimate scenario, the form reveals cost-related helper fields: the uninsured indicator, related service IDs (for review/editing as raw identifiers or text), and a numeric field for the user to type a totalEstimatedCost that matches what they describe in the message.
8. The user reviews the prefilled message, adds relevant clinical or financial details (e.g., tooth location, previous treatment, desired timeline), and enters or adjusts the totalEstimatedCost field.
9. The user confirms their name, email, phone, and preferred contact method, then submits the form.
10. The form validates required fields and message length, and ensures that uninsured-specific prompts (such as the totalEstimatedCost field) are either filled in or explicitly left blank according to the practice’s chosen rules.
11. The page calls submitContactSubmission with submissionType, subject, message, isUninsured, relatedServiceIds (as an array), totalEstimatedCost (if provided), and the contact fields.
12. On success, the confirmation panel is displayed with the backend’s confirmation message and status. The user can return to the homepage or start another inquiry from the same page.

Workflow 3: Edit a pre-filled insurance-related inquiry and change it to a general question
1. From insurance_payments.html, the user follows a link to contact.html with prefilled parameters (e.g., submissionType, subject, messageTemplate).
2. On load, contact.html fetches configuration via getContactFormConfig and then initializes the form fields from the URL parameters.
3. The user decides the inquiry should be a general question instead of a cost estimate and changes the submission type using the submission type selector.
4. The form reacts to the change by hiding cost-specific fields when the submission type no longer represents a cost estimate scenario.
5. The user adjusts the subject and message (for example, to ask about insurance coverage rules) and ensures their contact details are correct.
6. The user submits the form, which is validated and then sent via submitContactSubmission.
7. The confirmation panel appears after a successful response; the user may either leave the page or click an action to submit another inquiry, which resets the form while preserving configuration.

Workflow 4: Handle validation or backend errors during submission
1. The user fills out the contact form but leaves a required field empty (e.g., message or contact email) or exceeds the configured max_message_length.
2. When the user submits, client-side validation runs. Detected issues are shown in the validation-error-banner component with a concise, field-specific list of problems.
3. The user corrects the highlighted fields and attempts submission again.
4. If the backend returns an error (success is false), the submission-confirmation area is not shown; instead, an error state is surfaced in the validation-error-banner component, preserving the user’s form input.
5. The user can adjust any fields and retry submitting until a successful response is obtained or they navigate away from the page.
interactions: On page load, trigger a configuration fetch via getContactFormConfig. Once the configuration is loaded, initialize form state and apply any relevant URL parameters before the form is shown as ready for input.

When the user selects a submission type from the dynamically populated list, update internal form state, show or hide cost estimate–specific fields, and adjust helper text to match the selected inquiry type.

When URL parameter submissionType is present and matches one of the configured submission_types values, automatically select that option in the submission type selector; if it does not match, use the UI’s default selection logic instead.
When URL parameter subject is present, prefill the subject input field so the user can refine it.
When URL parameter messageTemplate is present, prefill the message textarea with its content. The user may edit or completely replace this text.

When URL parameter relatedServiceIds is present, parse the comma-separated string into an internal array and store it with the form state; allow the user to review and optionally edit these identifiers in a dedicated input area for related service IDs.

When URL parameter isUninsured is present, parse the value as a boolean and initialize the uninsured toggle or checkbox accordingly; subsequent user interaction with that control updates the isUninsured state.

As the user types into the message field, enforce the max_message_length received from getContactFormConfig by preventing additional input beyond the limit or by providing a live character counter that informs the user when the limit is reached.
When the user changes the preferred contact method, update internal state; if the user selects phone as preferred while contactPhone is empty, surface a validation warning when attempting to submit.

When the user enters a number into the totalEstimatedCost field, validate that the input is numeric and, if appropriate, non-negative. Store this value for inclusion in the submitContactSubmission call.

When the user submits the form, run client-side validation (required fields, email format, message length, and any additional rules). If validation fails, display messages in the validation-error-banner and prevent the submitContactSubmission call.
If validation passes, call submitContactSubmission with mapped form state and disable the submit button or show a loading state until a response is received.

On a successful submitContactSubmission response, save the returned contactSubmissionId and status in state, display the submission-confirmation-panel with the response message, and optionally hide or lock the form to avoid duplicate submissions.
On an unsuccessful submitContactSubmission response, display the error message returned (if any) in the validation-error-banner, keep the form data intact, and re-enable the submit control.
When the user clicks the "Return to homepage" link in the confirmation panel, navigate to index.html without additional parameters.

If the confirmation panel offers a "Submit another inquiry" action, reset the form fields to initial values (using the current configuration and, optionally, the original URL parameters) and return the page to the pre-submission state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page maintains state in memory for the current session and does not rely on localStorage or other persistent client-side storage. On initialization, the page performs the following steps in order: (1) parse the current URL for parameters submissionType, subject, relatedServiceIds, isUninsured, and messageTemplate; (2) call getContactFormConfig to retrieve submission_types, preferred_contact_methods, and max_message_length; (3) once configuration is available, construct an initial form state object that includes: selectedSubmissionType, subject, message, isUninsured (boolean), relatedServiceIds (array), totalEstimatedCost (empty or null), contactName, contactPhone, contactEmail, preferredContactMethod, validationErrors (empty), and submissionStatus (e.g., 'idle', 'submitting', 'submitted', 'error'). The selectedSubmissionType is set using the submissionType URL parameter only if it matches one of the values from configuration; otherwise, a default selection strategy (such as choosing the first configured submission type) is applied. The subject and message fields are initialized directly from subject and messageTemplate URL parameters if present. The relatedServiceIds string is split by commas and trimmed into an array; if no parameter is present, the array starts empty. The isUninsured parameter is interpreted as true or false according to its string value; if absent or invalid, the field uses a default (e.g., false) until the user changes it. During user interaction, changes to any form fields update this in-memory state. When the user submits, the state’s values are validated; if validation succeeds, the page transitions to a 'submitting' state and calls submitContactSubmission with the current state mapped to its parameters. The response from submitContactSubmission updates submissionStatus (e.g., 'new', 'in_review', 'responded', 'closed' as returned) and stores contactSubmissionId and the server-provided confirmation message. A 'submitted' state then drives rendering of the confirmation panel, while the form’s inputs can be disabled or hidden. URL parameters are not modified after initial load; starting a new inquiry on this page uses the existing configuration and may optionally reapply the original URL-derived defaults, but no new URL parameters are written by this page.

components.functionality: Coordinates overall page behavior. On page load, it parses URL parameters, triggers getContactFormConfig, and, once configuration is available, initializes the form state (including submission type, subject, message, uninsured flag, related service IDs, and preferred contact method). It tracks high-level view states such as 'loadingConfig', 'ready', 'submitting', 'submitted', and 'error'. It passes configuration and current state down to child components (contact-form, cost-estimate-helper-panel, validation-error-banner, submission-confirmation-panel) and receives events back to update the central state. When submitContactSubmission resolves, it stores contactSubmissionId, status, and the confirmation message, and switches the view to show the confirmation panel.

Renders the main contact and cost estimate form using configuration and state provided by the state manager. It includes: a submission type selector populated from configuration.submission_types; subject input; message textarea constrained by configuration.max_message_length; an uninsured toggle; a field for related service IDs (allowing the user to view or edit the identifiers passed in via URL); a numeric input for totalEstimatedCost; contact name, email, and phone fields; and a selector for preferred contact method populated from configuration.preferred_contact_methods. It displays inline validation feedback for required fields and prevents submission while invalid. When submitted, it emits a structured event containing the current form values so the state manager can call submitContactSubmission.

Provides context-sensitive guidance and additional inputs when the current submission type represents a cost estimate request. It listens to the selected submission type and isUninsured state from the state manager. When active, it may show explanatory text about how to request a cost estimate, encourage uninsured patients to provide detailed treatment scenarios and a typed totalEstimatedCost, and display or allow editing of related service IDs that were imported from URL parameters. It does not call any interfaces directly but updates the parent form state as the user interacts with its controls.
data_binding: ContactFormConfig (for any type-specific descriptions provided by configuration)

components.functionality: Displays a consolidated list of validation and submission errors at the top (or another prominent area) of the page. It receives a list of error messages and/or field-level error codes from the state manager after client-side validation fails or when submitContactSubmission returns an error or an unexpected response. It allows the user to dismiss transient error messages while keeping field-level indicators within the form. This component does not perform validation itself; it purely reflects the current validation state.

Tracks the current length of the message textarea and compares it to the max_message_length supplied by getContactFormConfig. It displays the current character count and remaining characters allowed, and can indicate when the limit is reached. It ensures that input beyond the configured limit is either disallowed or clearly flagged, keeping the main contact-form component simpler by isolating the counting logic.

Renders after a successful submitContactSubmission response. It shows the backend-provided confirmation message, the current ContactSubmission status, and may optionally display the contactSubmissionId for reference. It provides actions to return to the homepage (navigating to index.html) and to start another inquiry (which triggers a reset of the form state via the state manager). This component is only visible when the page state indicates a successful submission.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a max-width content container (~1140–1200px). The row of four circular service cards suggests four equal-width columns on desktop.
layout.layout_pattern: The global site header, hero, and circular service cards remain unchanged at the top, occupying roughly the upper half of the initial viewport. The Contact & Cost Estimates page content begins directly beneath this band as a centered content column. At the top of this column: a large centered H1 and short subheading, followed by a brief intro paragraph. Beneath that, a state-managed interaction region holds the validation error banner (when present), the contact form as the main focus, a cost-estimate helper panel alongside it on desktop, the character counter embedded in the form, and a submission confirmation panel that replaces the form after successful submission.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The global site header, hero, and circular service cards remain unchanged at the top, occupying roughly the upper half of the initial viewport. The Contact & Cost Estimates page content begins directly beneath this band as a centered content column. At the top of this column: a large centered H1 and short subheading, followed by a brief intro paragraph. Beneath that, a state-managed interaction region holds the validation error banner (when present), the contact form as the main focus, a cost-estimate helper panel alongside it on desktop, the character counter embedded in the form, and a submission confirmation panel that replaces the form after successful submission.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content container follows a 12-column, max-width ~1140–1200px grid. Within that, the interactive region for this page uses about 70% of the viewport width on large screens. The form occupies roughly 65–70% of this region on the left, while the helper panel uses the remaining 30–35% on the right. Vertical spacing adheres to the 8px base scale: 32–48px between major blocks (H1 to intro, intro to form area, form area to subsequent content, etc.), and 16–24px inside components. On mobile, the layout collapses into a single column with the helper panel stacking below the form/confirmation panel, maintaining generous vertical spacing between elements.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page keeps an editorial, moderately spacious feel. A prominent H1 and subheading sit with ample whitespace, followed by a clean, well-grouped form that avoids cramping by using clear vertical separation between field groups. The helper panel is text-light, focusing on concise bullet points or short paragraphs with icons to avoid overwhelming users while they fill the form. The error banner and confirmation panel are compact but clearly separated from the form, using strong visual cues without flooding the screen with text.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow continues the mockup’s Z-pattern starting above, but within this page section it becomes a refined top-down flow with lateral emphasis: the eye drops from the hero band to the centered H1, then to the intro copy, and down into the form area. Within the form band, the eye starts at the left-dominant form, then glances to the right at the helper panel for support. When errors occur, the eye is intercepted by the red-tinted error banner above the form. After submission, the confirmation panel occupies the same visual anchor position the form had, keeping the primary focus centered while the helper panel remains a secondary right-hand reference on desktop.

layout.overall_description: Below the visually rich header, hero, and row of circular service cards, the Contact & Cost Estimates page content appears as a centered, max-width band against a calm, light background. At the top of this band, a large serif H1 reading something like “Contact & Cost Estimates” is centered and spans about 60–70% of the content width, with generous 32–40px breathing room above and below. A smaller sans-serif welcome subheading is centered directly beneath it, followed by a short left-aligned introductory paragraph that explains the purpose of the form. Roughly 40–48px below this text block, a state-managed interaction zone begins. When there are validation issues, a full-width error banner appears first, stretching across the central content column with a modest drop shadow and clear iconography. Below that, the main contact form sits as a subtle card on the left two-thirds of the column, with soft corners, a light surface, and comfortable vertical spacing between fields. To its right, a narrower helper panel card aligns to the top of the form, slightly more colorful with brand greens/teals and simple icons, guiding patients on how to describe their treatment and cost questions. The message field in the form includes a small character counter tucked into the bottom-right area, maintaining the tidy, professional look. Upon successful submission, the form area is replaced by a confirmation panel in the same left-side footprint, keeping the central focus while the helper panel remains in place to suggest next steps or other ways to contact the practice. On smaller screens, all these elements collapse into a single linear column: H1 and intro, then error banner if present, then the form or confirmation panel, and finally the helper panel, each separated by 24–32px to preserve the spacious, trustworthy, medical-corporate aesthetic.
