component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-page-state-manager occupies the central content column directly beneath the introductory text (which includes the H1, subheading, and short intro paragraph). It spans approximately 70% of the viewport width within the max-width container and is horizontally centered, aligning with the grid used by the rest of the page. Visually, it does not present its own borders or background; instead, it acts as an invisible wrapper that defines the vertical zone where the validation-error-banner, contact-form, cost-estimate-helper-panel, form-character-counter, and submission-confirmation-panel appear. The top of this state-managed region begins about 40px below the intro paragraph. Inside this region, the error banner (when present) is positioned at the top, followed by the form plus helper panel side-by-side, or the confirmation panel plus helper panel side-by-side after submission. On mobile, the state manager still spans the full width of the content container, but all child components stack vertically, preserving 24–32px of separation between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a normal, block-level layout wrapper with no fixed or sticky behavior; it simply controls which child components are mounted or hidden while maintaining the same overall column footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form is the main interactive card in the state-managed region and sits on the left side of the content area. On desktop, it occupies roughly 65–70% of the available width inside the central column, aligning with 8–9 grid columns out of 12. It is vertically positioned 24–32px below the validation-error-banner (if present) or directly below the intro paragraph/state-manager top when no errors are shown. The form appears as a light, slightly elevated card with a subtle drop shadow, rounded corners (6–8px radius), and 24px internal padding on all sides. Form fields are arranged in a clear vertical stack with 16–24px spacing between field groups, keeping the layout open and easy to scan. Labels sit above inputs, and any inline field-level messages align beneath their respective fields, not breaking the overall vertical rhythm. The top of the form aligns horizontally with the top of the cost-estimate-helper-panel to its right, creating a cohesive band. The bottom of the form extends further down than the helper panel, forming a taller left column that anchors the page. On tablet and mobile widths (e.g., below ~768px), the form expands to nearly 100% of the content width and the helper panel drops below it, but the form’s internal spacing and padding remain consistent with the desktop styling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level component positioned within the normal document flow; it adjusts responsively from a left-side dominant card on desktop to a full-width stacked card on narrower screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cost-estimate-helper-panel is positioned to the immediate right of the contact-form, forming a secondary, vertical card that begins at the same top edge as the form. On desktop, it occupies roughly 30–35% of the central column width (about 3–4 grid columns out of 12) and sits with a 24–32px horizontal gap between its left edge and the right edge of the form, maintaining breathable spacing without feeling disconnected. The panel appears as a slightly more colorful card using brand greens/teals or a soft gradient, with a subtle drop shadow and rounded corners that match the form. It has 24px internal padding and a vertical stack of concise content: a small heading at the top, possibly accompanied by a circular gradient icon aligned to the left, followed by 2–4 short paragraphs or bullet points explaining what information helps generate accurate cost estimates, including uninsured scenarios. The panel’s height is determined by its content and is typically shorter than the form, giving it a balanced but clearly secondary presence. On tablet and mobile, this panel becomes full-width and moves below the form (or confirmation panel), separated by 24–32px of vertical space, still centered within the content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, responsive block-level component. It sits flush to the right of the form on larger screens and reflows beneath the form/confirmation panel in a single-column stack on smaller screens, maintaining its card-like appearance.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The validation-error-banner appears at the very top of the state-managed region, directly below the intro text block and spanning the same width as the central content column (approximately 70% of the viewport width, centered). It has a horizontal orientation similar to a full-width alert bar: a soft but noticeable red or warm warning background, a left-aligned icon (e.g., an exclamation within a circle), and short, bold sans-serif text explaining the presence of form errors. The banner has 16–20px of vertical padding and 24px of horizontal padding, with rounded corners and a slight drop shadow to match the site’s friendly, card-based aesthetic. There is a 24px vertical gap between the bottom of the banner and the top of the contact-form, preserving separation and avoiding visual clutter. When no errors are present, this space collapses and the form moves up. On mobile, the banner simply scales to full container width and maintains the same padding proportions, stacking its icon and message text if needed for legibility.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow of the content; it appears and disappears based on validation state but does not use sticky behavior. When visible, it pushes the form and helper panel downward while maintaining consistent spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The form-character-counter is a small, unobtrusive utility element embedded inside the contact-form near the message textarea. It aligns to the bottom-right corner of the message field’s content area, either within the field’s border or just below it, depending on the final control style. Horizontally, it is right-aligned to the message field’s right edge; vertically, it sits about 4–8px below the bottom of the textarea’s input area, maintaining the 8px base spacing scale. Its text is small and in a neutral, low-contrast sans-serif style so it remains readable but does not compete with labels or helper text. There is a 12–16px gap between the counter and the next element below (such as the submit button row), ensuring it doesn’t feel cramped. On mobile, the counter remains in the same relative bottom-right position within the form’s layout, adjusting fluidly with the width of the message field.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fully contained within the form’s layout as an inline utility; it moves and resizes with the message field, without independent positioning or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission-confirmation-panel occupies the exact horizontal footprint where the contact-form normally resides. When the form is successfully submitted, the confirmation panel replaces the form in the left portion of the state-managed region while the cost-estimate-helper-panel remains on the right, preserving the overall two-column balance on desktop. The panel spans roughly 65–70% of the central content column, aligning to the left just as the form did, and sits about 24–32px below the validation-error-banner area (which is typically hidden in this state). It appears as a friendly card with a light background, subtle drop shadow, and rounded corners consistent with the form. Inside, a prominent serif heading (e.g., “Thank you for contacting us”) is left-aligned at the top, followed by a short sans-serif paragraph confirming receipt and outlining next steps (estimated response time, what to expect regarding cost estimates, etc.). A small success icon (possibly a circular gradient checkmark) can appear to the left of the heading or above it for visual reassurance. Vertical spacing within the panel uses 16–24px gaps between elements, keeping the layout airy and easy to read. On tablet and mobile, when this panel replaces the form, it expands to nearly full width of the content column and stacks above the helper panel, with 24–32px separation between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level component that is mutually exclusive with the contact-form within the state-managed region. It occupies the same spatial slot as the form, reflowing responsively in the same way (side-by-side with the helper panel on desktop, stacked above it on smaller screens).

name: Book Appointment
title: Book Appointment
description: Page for patients to request dental appointments or consultations by selecting patient context, visit reason, location, provider, timing preferences, and providing contact details. Uses live options and availability from backend interfaces and submits an appointment request for staff confirmation.

core_features: Initialize booking context using URL parameters and load dynamic options (patient types, age groups, services, locations, dentists, insurance plans, default selections) via getBookingFormOptions.

Let users select and adjust core patient context: patient type (new or existing), age group, and visit reason/service, filtered according to age, new-patient eligibility, and any promotion or orthodontic context.
Allow selection and adjustment of clinic location and (optionally) specific dentist, with dentist options filtered by location, service compatibility, pediatric suitability, and activity status.
Provide date and time preference controls (date range, weekday/weekend, part of day, optional start/end time range) and search matching appointment slots through getAvailableAppointmentSlots.
Display available appointment slots and let users pick one slot or instead indicate a preferred date/time when no exact slot is chosen or available.
Capture required patient and contact details (patient name, contact name, relationship, phone, email, age when relevant), plus notes and insurance/promotion information.
Submit the appointment or consultation request using submitAppointmentRequest and show a confirmation state with a summary and navigation options.

Support bookings initiated from other pages (services, promotions, dentists, locations, insurance, new patients) with contextual pre-selections (service, dentist, location, insurance plan, promotion, age group, time-of-day preference).
Provide navigation shortcuts: link to insurance & payments information, link to contact page prefilled for online booking questions, and back-to-home navigation from the confirmation state.
user_workflows: Workflow 1: Default booking from site navigation. Step 1: User opens appointment_booking.html without special URL parameters; Step 2: Page reads URL, calls getBookingFormOptions with sourcePage set appropriately and no preselected IDs; Step 3: User chooses patient type and age group, then selects a visit reason from the services list filtered by age group and patient type; Step 4: User selects a location and optionally a dentist filtered by that location and service; Step 5: User selects a desired date range (e.g., upcoming days) and part of day, then triggers a slot search; Step 6: Page calls getAvailableAppointmentSlots with chosen filters and displays matching slots; Step 7: User selects a slot, fills in patient/contact details and insurance/promotion details if applicable; Step 8: User submits the form, page calls submitAppointmentRequest; Step 9: Page shows confirmation summary with an option to return to the home page.

Workflow 2: Weekday evening cleaning next week. Step 1: User navigates from a services or pricing page where a cleaning-related serviceId is passed in the URL; Step 2: Page calls getBookingFormOptions with preselectedServiceId set from the URL, so the visit reason is preselected; Step 3: User selects a location and optionally keeps or changes the preselected service; Step 4: In the date/time preferences, user selects a date range corresponding to next week and sets partOfDay to 'evening', optionally setting earliestStartTime and latestStartTime for a refined range; Step 5: Page calls getAvailableAppointmentSlots with those filters and displays only evening slots in the selected week; Step 6: User chooses a desired slot and completes the contact details; Step 7: User submits, calling submitAppointmentRequest with appointmentSlotId set to the chosen slot.

Workflow 3: Saturday pediatric visit after late morning. Step 1: User arrives with ageGroup set to 'child' (optionally from a new patients or pediatric content page) and possibly a pediatric service preselected; Step 2: Page calls getBookingFormOptions with preselectedAgeGroup and optional preselectedServiceId; Step 3: User confirms 'child' age group and selects a pediatric-appropriate service; Step 4: User chooses a location and optionally a pediatric-capable dentist; Step 5: In date/time preferences, user restricts daysOfWeek to Saturday and sets earliestStartTime to a time after late morning, optionally keeping partOfDay as 'afternoon'; Step 6: Page calls getAvailableAppointmentSlots with is_saturday filter implied by daysOfWeek and the time filters; Step 7: User selects one of the Saturday child-friendly slots and completes the form; Step 8: submitAppointmentRequest is called with isEmergency set according to user input (typically false) and the chosen slot and dentist/location.

Workflow 4: New patient child exam on weekday afternoons. Step 1: User clicks a booking link for a new child patient exam on the new patients page, passing sourcePage, serviceId, ageGroup='child', and possibly isNewPatientPromotion; Step 2: Page calls getBookingFormOptions with those preselected parameters, which may pre-fill patient_type as new_patient and a specific recommended service; Step 3: User confirms patient type 'new' and age group 'child', and can adjust the service if needed; Step 4: User selects a location and optionally a pediatric specialist; Step 5: In date/time preferences, user selects desired weekdays and partOfDay='afternoon', optionally setting a multi-day dateRange; Step 6: Page calls getAvailableAppointmentSlots with those filters; Step 7: User selects a suitable weekday afternoon child-friendly slot and completes contact details including child age; Step 8: Page uses submitAppointmentRequest including patientAge and isNewPatientPromotion when present; Step 9: Confirmation state shows the requested exam details.

Workflow 5: Invisalign or promotion-based consultation. Step 1: User clicks 'Book consultation' from an orthodontics or promotion detail page, passing sourcePage, serviceId tied to a consultation, promotionId, isNewPatientPromotion, optional partOfDay, and potentially preselectedLocationId or preselectedDentistId; Step 2: Page reads URL parameters and calls getBookingFormOptions with preselected service, promotion, location, dentist, insurance, and age group; Step 3: Form initializes with those preselected values and indicates that a promotion is attached when applicable; Step 4: User may adjust patient type, age group, or service (remaining within valid options), and confirms or changes location and dentist; Step 5: Date/time preferences may default partOfDay from URL (e.g., 'evening'), which the user can change; Step 6: Page calls getAvailableAppointmentSlots with filters that include the serviceId, promotion context only used later by submitAppointmentRequest, and the chosen timing; Step 7: User selects a slot or enters a preferred date/time if no exact slot is selected; Step 8: User enters contact details and submits; Step 9: submitAppointmentRequest includes promotionId and isNewPatientPromotion flags; Step 10: Confirmation summary shows that the consultation is tied to the promotion.

Workflow 6: User has accepted insurance plan preselected. Step 1: User comes from the insurance & payments page with insurancePlanId and sourcePage in the URL; Step 2: Page calls getBookingFormOptions with preselectedInsurancePlanId, which preselects the insurance plan in the booking form; Step 3: User chooses patient type, age group, service, location, dentist, and date/time filters as desired; Step 4: After choosing a slot and filling out contact details, user submits; Step 5: submitAppointmentRequest includes insurancePlanId, allowing staff to associate the request with the correct insurance plan; Step 6: From the form, user can also follow a link back to the insurance & payments page if they need more information before submitting.

Workflow 7: User needs help and switches to contact page. Step 1: User begins filling out the booking form but has a question about online booking; Step 2: User clicks the 'Have questions? Contact us' link below the form; Step 3: Browser navigates to contact.html with submissionType and subject URL parameters set according to specification, without submitting an appointment request; Step 4: User can then send a general question from the contact page instead of completing booking.
interactions: On page load, a component reads URL parameters (sourcePage, serviceId, dentistId, locationId, insurancePlanId, promotionId, ageGroup, isNewPatientPromotion, partOfDay) and triggers getBookingFormOptions with corresponding preselected* parameters. The returned options populate dropdowns and form controls, and default selections are applied to the initial state.

When the user changes patient type or age group, the visit reason/service options are filtered client-side based on service attributes (such as applies_to_adult, applies_to_child, is_new_patient_only) from getBookingFormOptions. If the current service selection becomes invalid for the new context, it is cleared and the user is prompted to choose again.

When the user selects or changes a service, the dentist and location lists may be filtered client-side based on dentist.active, dentist.specialties, is_pediatric_specialist, available_service_ids, and locations that support relevant hours (such as evening or Saturday), using only the data from getBookingFormOptions. Any dentist or location selection that becomes incompatible is cleared and requires re-selection.

When the user selects or changes a location, the dentist selector updates to show only dentists whose primary_location_id or other_location_ids include that locationId, using data from getBookingFormOptions. If a preselected dentistId is not associated with the new location, that dentist selection is cleared.

When the user selects or changes a dentist, the system retains the chosen location if compatible. If the selected dentist has a primary location different from the currently selected location and does not practice at that location, the component prompts the user to either switch to the dentist's location or choose a different dentist.

When the user adjusts date-related filters (dateRange start/end) or day-of-week preferences, these values are stored in component state and can also be reflected in the URL query string to support back/forward navigation and shareable links.

When the user sets part-of-day filter or explicit earliestStartTime and latestStartTime values, the filter state is updated and used the next time the user searches for slots. If partOfDay is preselected from the URL, that value is prefilled into the filter controls and remains editable.

When the user clicks a 'Search availability' or equivalent action in the date/time preference area, the component calls getAvailableAppointmentSlots with the current filters: locationId, dentistId (optional), serviceId, ageGroup, patientType, dateRange, daysOfWeek, partOfDay, earliestStartTime, latestStartTime, and includeFullyBooked (set based on a user toggle or a default behavior). The response is rendered as a list or grouped display of AppointmentSlot options.

When the user clicks on an available appointment slot from the list, the chosen slot_id is stored in the booking state and any previously entered preferred date/time text is cleared, ensuring that only one of slot_id or preferredDateTime will be sent to submitAppointmentRequest.

If no suitable slot is available or the user prefers flexibility, the user can enter a preferred date/time field instead of selecting a slot. The component enforces that either an appointmentSlotId or a preferredDateTime (but not both) will be sent to the submitAppointmentRequest interface.

When the user completes the patient and contact details form (patient_name, contact_name, relationship, phone, email, and optional patient_age and notes) and clicks the submit button, the booking form validates required fields, ensures serviceId and locationId are set, and then calls submitAppointmentRequest with all current selections including insurancePlanId, promotionId, isNewPatientPromotion flag, and isEmergency flag derived from a checkbox or selection.

On successful submitAppointmentRequest response where success is true, the page transitions to a confirmation state. The component displays the confirmation_message and summary (patient_name, service_name, location_name, dentist_name, date_time) from the response instead of the editable form, and disables further form submission for that request.
On the confirmation state, when the user clicks 'Back to Home', the page navigates to index.html without preserving the booking form state, as specified in outgoing navigation.

Within the booking form state, when the user clicks 'See insurance & payment options', the browser navigates to insurance_payments.html, allowing the user to review accepted plans and costs. Returning to appointment_booking.html from there can again prefill insurancePlanId through URL parameters.

When the user clicks the 'Have questions? Contact us' link below the form, the browser navigates to contact.html with URL parameters submissionType and subject set as described in the outgoing navigation specification, without invoking submitAppointmentRequest.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL parameters and in-memory component state, avoiding localStorage. On initial load, URL parameters (sourcePage, serviceId, dentistId, locationId, insurancePlanId, promotionId, ageGroup, isNewPatientPromotion, partOfDay) are parsed and normalized. These values are mapped to getBookingFormOptions parameters (sourcePage to sourcePage; serviceId to preselectedServiceId; dentistId to preselectedDentistId; locationId to preselectedLocationId; insurancePlanId to preselectedInsurancePlanId; promotionId to preselectedPromotionId; ageGroup to preselectedAgeGroup). The response's default_selections object is merged with any explicit URL-driven choices to form the initial booking state. When the user changes certain key selections (such as service, location, dentist, partOfDay, patientType, ageGroup, or dateRange), the page may update the browser's query string using the History API (for example, changing serviceId in the URL when the user selects a different service) so that the current context is shareable and survives reloads. The confirmation state is distinguished from the editable form state by internal state flags that depend on the result of submitAppointmentRequest; the URL does not need a separate parameter for confirmation, but can optionally include an appointmentRequestId if desired by the implementation, without changing interface contracts. The getAvailableAppointmentSlots call relies solely on the current in-memory filters, which are themselves initialized from the URL where applicable (for example, partOfDay from the URL influences the initial filter control and thus the parameters sent to getAvailableAppointmentSlots). Promotion-related flags in the URL (promotionId, isNewPatientPromotion) are preserved in booking state and passed through to submitAppointmentRequest without modification so that the backend can honor promotion constraints. If the user navigates away (e.g., to insurance_payments.html or contact.html) and returns to appointment_booking.html with new URL parameters, the page re-runs the initialization logic, calling getBookingFormOptions again and re-establishing state only from the new URL and API responses.

components.functionality: Reads URL parameters on page load, calls getBookingFormOptions with the corresponding preselected parameters, and initializes the shared booking state (patient type, age group, service, location, dentist, insurance plan, promotion) using the returned options and default_selections.

Manages selection of patient type, age group, and visit reason/service. Filters services based on age group, patient type, and new-patient-only constraints using data from getBookingFormOptions. Updates booking state and propagates changes to dependent components (location/dentist choices and slot search filters).

Allows the user to choose a clinic location and optionally a specific dentist. Filters dentists by activity, specialties, pediatric suitability, supported services, and locations based on the chosen service, age group, and location using the data from getBookingFormOptions. Resolves conflicts when selected dentists and locations are incompatible and updates booking state accordingly.

Captures date range, days of week (including weekday/weekend preferences), part of day, and optional earliest/latest start time inputs that define when the patient would like to be seen. Initializes partOfDay from URL when present and keeps these preferences in sync with the query string as needed. Prepares the parameters for getAvailableAppointmentSlots.

Calls getAvailableAppointmentSlots using the current booking context (locationId, dentistId, serviceId, ageGroup, patientType) and date/time filters from the date-time-preferences component. Displays the returned appointment slots, including indicators like part_of_day, is_saturday, is_weekend, and is_child_friendly, and lets the user select one slot for the appointment.

Provides an alternative input for the user to specify a preferred date/time when they do not select a specific slot. Enforces mutual exclusivity between a chosen appointmentSlotId and a manual preferredDateTime value before submission.

Displays and manages the selection of an insurance plan and shows whether a promotion is tied to the booking. Preselects insurance and promotion based on getBookingFormOptions defaults and URL parameters. Ensures promotionId and isNewPatientPromotion flags are tracked for submitAppointmentRequest.

Collects patient name, contact name, relationship, phone, email, optional patient age (especially for child appointments), and free-text notes. Validates required fields and formats, and stores them in the appointment request state prior to submission.
Allows the user to indicate whether the request is an emergency by toggling a control. Updates the isEmergency flag in the appointment request state so it can be passed to submitAppointmentRequest.

Coordinates validation of all booking sections (patient context, location/dentist, timing preferences or slot selection, patient/contact details, insurance/promotion flags, emergency status) and, on successful validation, calls submitAppointmentRequest with all required parameters and appropriate null/empty values for optional ones. Handles success and error responses and updates the page state to show confirmation or inline errors.

Displays the confirmation_message and summary data returned by submitAppointmentRequest (patient_name, service_name, location_name, dentist_name, date_time) when success is true. Replaces or overlays the main form when in confirmation state and provides navigation options such as 'Back to Home'.

Provides a link from the booking form to the insurance_payments.html page so users can review insurance and payment options before or during booking. May optionally construct a URL that preserves current context if needed.
Provides a link to contact.html with URL parameters for submissionType and subject pre-filled for online booking questions, allowing users to seek help without submitting an appointment request.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a max-width content container (~1140–1200px). The row of four circular service cards suggests four equal-width columns on desktop.
layout.layout_pattern: All booking components sit within a centered main content column beneath the existing hero/nav band from the mockup. The flow is strictly top-to-bottom, with the booking journey divided into clear, labeled sections: patient context first, then location/provider, then date & time preferences with availability, then fallback preferred time, then insurance messaging, then contact details, and finally submission. State-specific elements (initializer, confirmation) temporarily overlay or replace this central column but respect the same centered alignment.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All booking components sit within a centered main content column beneath the existing hero/nav band from the mockup. The flow is strictly top-to-bottom, with the booking journey divided into clear, labeled sections: patient context first, then location/provider, then date & time preferences with availability, then fallback preferred time, then insurance messaging, then contact details, and finally submission. State-specific elements (initializer, confirmation) temporarily overlay or replace this central column but respect the same centered alignment.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column uses roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop (within a ~1140–1200px max-width container), centered with generous side gutters. Each major section (patient context, location/provider, time preferences, results, contact details, submit) is separated vertically by about 32–48px. Within sections, fields are arranged in 2-column layouts where helpful, but no more than two dense rows are visible without clear spacing to maintain a comfortable, non-cramped feel. Vertical stacking dominates, with only short horizontal groupings inside sections (e.g., location vs dentist, first/last name).
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate and patient-friendly. Each section has a short heading or label row and then 1–2 rows of fields, with 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px spacing between individual controls. The availability list is the densest area but still uses card-like rows and clear grouping to avoid a wall of text. Long forms are broken into clearly segmented blocks to preserve an editorial, approachable feel consistent with the mockup’s spacious hero and large typography.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The main flow is vertical, but with local Z-patterns: section headings and primary controls start left, then eyes move right to secondary controls (e.g., emergency toggle) or supporting text, then drop down to the next block. Users start at the top-centered page title, immediately move into the patient-context section, then scan left-to-right across location and provider, then down into date filters and rightward across time preferences and results. Supporting items like insurance info and contact questions sit toward the right or bottom-right of the main column, acting as secondary off-ramps that do not compete with the primary booking flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing hero/nav and circular feature band, the page transitions into a calm, centered booking column that mirrors the mockup’s editorial style. A large, centered “Book an Appointment” heading and a short, friendly subheading span roughly two-thirds of the page width, with generous white space above and below. Directly beneath, the booking form unfolds as a sequence of broad, card-like sections, each with subtle shadow and soft edges to echo the site’s traditional yet approachable aesthetic. The patient context block appears first, followed by a split row where location selection sits on the left and provider selection on the right. Further down, date and time preferences are grouped above a scrollable list of available slots, arranged in tidy rows. A smaller band offers a way to specify an exact preferred time if no listed slot works. Insurance reassurance and a link to detailed insurance info appear as a right-leaning callout before the more personal contact details section, which again adopts a two-column field layout where appropriate. At the bottom of this main stack, a wide, centered submission bar contains the primary green “Request Appointment” button. During loading, a semi-opaque overlay centers a spinner over the column, and after submission, a confirmation panel replaces the form with a simple success message and follow-up details, all still locked into that central, well-spaced column that reflects the original mockup’s balance of trustworthiness and clarity.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The initializer appears as a full-viewport, semi-opaque white overlay that sits on top of the entire page, including the hero area and the central booking column. It dims the content below while keeping the overall teal/green brand colors faintly visible. At the center of the viewport, aligned both vertically and horizontally, a compact card (about 320px wide) with a subtle drop shadow contains a circular spinner icon (using the same gradient style as the site’s circular cards) and a short loading message in sans-serif text. The overlay edge aligns with the browser edges rather than the content container, ensuring that the user’s focus is constrained to the center of the screen until initialization completes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay anchored to the viewport, covering 100% width and height; fades in on page load and fades out once initial data is ready, revealing the underlying centered layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This section is the first functional block in the booking page’s central column, sitting directly below the page’s H1 and intro text, with about 32–40px of vertical space separating them. It appears as a full-width card within the central container, spanning roughly 65% of the viewport width on large screens and centered horizontally. The card has 24px internal padding, a soft shadow, and a subtle light background to distinguish it from the page background. At the top-left of the card is a serif or bold sans-serif section heading like “Patient Information”, with the body of the form arranged below in one or two horizontal lines: for example, patient type (new/existing), patient name or selector, and relationship fields. Controls in a given row align left and stretch to about half-width each, with 16–24px gaps between them. To maintain the Z-pattern, the left side of the top row holds the main control (e.g., patient type), leaving the upper-right corner visually available for the emergency toggle component while still feeling part of this section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a standard block in the document, expanding vertically as fields dictate; on narrow screens, fields stack to full width while maintaining the same vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This section sits directly below the patient context card with about 32px of vertical space, forming the next segment of the central column. It appears as a wide card similar in style and width (around 65% of the viewport) to create a continuous stacked rhythm. Inside, a short heading row runs along the top, with a label like “Choose Location & Dentist”. Directly underneath, the content is split into two equal columns within the card: on the left, a location selector block (dropdown or tiles) occupies about 48–50% of the card width; on the right, a dentist/provider selector aligned to the right column occupies a similar width. Both columns are balanced vertically, with 16–24px horizontal spacing between them. This side-by-side arrangement reinforces the left-to-right scan of the Z-pattern: first location on the left, then dentist on the right, before the user’s gaze drops to the next section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section; switches to a stacked layout on smaller screens, with location appearing above dentist, each taking full width with vertical spacing preserved.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned beneath the location/dentist selector with 32–40px of separation, this section is slightly taller and visually richer, reflecting its importance. It spans the same central width as prior sections and uses a card style with 24px internal padding. A heading like “Preferred Date & Time” anchors the top-left. Below the heading, the upper half of the card is arranged into 2–3 logical groupings across: for example, a date picker occupying the full left half, and smaller preference controls (time of day, days of week, flexibility options) aligned to the right half. Controls are grouped into lines so that the eye moves from the date picker on the left, across to time-of-day options on the right, then down. The bottom edge of this section aligns visually with the top of the availability results list, creating the sense that filters feed directly into the results below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block with responsive behavior: on large screens, groups form two horizontal bands; on small screens, all controls stack vertically while preserving clear grouping and 16–24px spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data list sits immediately below the date-time preferences section, with only a modest gap of about 24–32px to reinforce their connection. The results area extends across the full width of the central column (matching the card width above), but it may appear visually as a distinct card with its own subtle border and slightly stronger shadow, signaling dynamic content. At the top of this panel, a narrow header row shows a title like “Available Times” on the left and possibly a concise summary of filters or a count on the right. Below, appointment options are displayed as vertically stacked rows or cards, each spanning nearly the full width of the panel with 12–16px of vertical spacing between them. Each row organizes information left-to-right—date and time on the left, provider/location confirmation in the center, and a small “Select” button aligned right—mirroring the Z scan within each row. The panel’s height grows with content but generally occupies a noticeable portion of the viewport (roughly the middle third), encouraging scrolling when there are many options.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable block; the list itself can scroll as the page scrolls. On long lists, the page uses normal document scrolling rather than an internal scroll area to keep behavior predictable and consistent with the site’s traditional feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly beneath the availability results, with around 24–32px of vertical separation, this component appears as a slimmer, more understated card spanning about 60–65% of the viewport width, centered like the others. It is introduced with a short, left-aligned label such as “Can’t find a suitable time?” followed by an inline set of fields: a date input and a time window input (from/to), possibly arranged in a single horizontal row on larger screens, taking up roughly two-thirds of the card’s width, with the remainder right-aligned explanatory text or a subtle hint. The visual hierarchy is softer than the main availability panel, using a lighter border and less pronounced shadow, indicating that this is an alternative option. The user’s eye naturally drops from the more prominent list of available slots to this fallback entry area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline block within the central column; fields stack vertically on small screens, with the label above and inputs below, preserving comfortable spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This section is located below the preferred datetime input but above the main contact details form, separated by about 32px of vertical space. It appears as a compact, horizontally oriented callout card aligned with the main column but visually slightly narrower (about 55–60% of viewport width) and shifted subtly toward the right side of the central column to reinforce its secondary nature while still feeling integrated. The card uses a stronger brand green/teal accent stripe along its left edge or header and a soft gradient background that ties into the site’s circular icon treatments. Inside, a brief reassurance message about accepted insurance is left-aligned with a concise heading and one or two lines of body text, leaving space at the bottom-right or inline right for the insurance-info-link component. This section is visually distinct enough to catch attention but smaller and less tall than the core form sections, so it doesn’t interrupt the booking flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block, non-sticky; on narrow screens, it expands to full width and realigns centrally, with the accent stripe and text stacking vertically.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Located beneath the insurance promotion callout with 32–40px of separation, this is one of the taller and more information-dense sections. It spans the same width as the main form sections (around 65% of viewport width) and uses a card with generous 24px padding. A heading like “Your Contact Details” or “How We Can Reach You” anchors the top-left. Below, the form fields are organized into a two-column layout where feasible: for instance, first and last name share a row, email and phone share another, with fields each taking roughly 48% of the card width and 16–24px between columns. Additional fields (e.g., notes or special requests) can stretch the full width and sit in their own rows. Vertical spacing between rows is consistent (16–24px), preserving readability. The overall height of this card, combined with clear grouping and label hierarchy, keeps it approachable despite containing more fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block that grows vertically with content; on smaller screens, it collapses to a single-column stack, with each field at full width and consistent vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The emergency toggle is visually and functionally associated with the top of the booking journey. It appears within the same horizontal band as the patient-context-form heading, but aligned to the far right edge of that card. Inside the patient context card’s header row, the left side carries the section title, while on the right the emergency toggle sits as a compact pill-shaped switch or checkbox accompanied by a concise label such as “This is an emergency” in sans-serif text. The control occupies roughly 20–25% of the card’s width, with enough padding around it to avoid crowding the heading. Its placement at the top-right of this first section utilizes the Z-pattern: users see the title on the left, then naturally note the emergency option on the right before moving down into the fields. When active, the toggle could adopt a more vivid brand green fill to increase visibility, but its physical size remains modest.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the patient-context section’s header row; it moves to a position just below the heading and aligned left on narrow screens to maintain legibility.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component forms the closing section of the booking column and sits immediately below the patient contact details form, with about 32–40px of vertical space between. It appears as a wide, low-profile bar or card spanning the same width as the main form sections. Inside, the primary call-to-action button—styled in the brand’s strong green with subtle shadow and rounded corners—sits centered horizontally or slightly right-of-center, large enough to feel inviting (e.g., 48px tall with ample horizontal padding). To its left or beneath it on smaller screens, a short line of explanatory text (e.g., “We’ll review your request and confirm by phone or email”) uses lighter-weight sans-serif, giving reassurance without overcrowding. The overall height of this controller is modest, but generous vertical padding (16–24px) ensures the button feels tappable and not cramped. The user’s eye naturally falls from the contact form down to this bar as the clear final action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block at the end of the form flow; it scrolls with content and is not sticky, matching the more traditional, non-techy feel of the site.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Upon successful submission, the confirmation panel replaces the main form stack within the central column, occupying roughly the same width (about 60–70% of viewport) and appearing centered both horizontally and vertically within the visible area of the page where possible. It takes the form of a spacious card with strong visual weight: a prominent circular success icon (checkmark inside a gradient circle that echoes the site’s hero cards) appears at the top center. Below, a large serif-style confirmation heading is centered, followed by a few short lines of explanatory text detailing what happens next (e.g., staff will call to confirm). Additional details like the requested date/time and location may be presented in a tidy 1–2 column summary block beneath, with clear labels and generous spacing. The panel’s height roughly fills the central portion of the viewport without feeling cramped, and surrounding white space reinforces a sense of completion and calm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the form content in the normal document flow; centered within the main container. If the page is scrolled, the user may need to scroll slightly to see it fully, but no overlay or fixed positioning is used to keep behavior predictable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This navigation link is visually integrated into the insurance-promotion-section rather than standing alone. Within that callout card, it appears aligned to the bottom-right or inline at the end of the promotional text, styled as an underlined or subtly accented text link in the brand’s teal/green. It occupies only a small portion of the card’s width—effectively one line of text—and sits with about 8–12px of space above it to separate it from the descriptive copy. Its position at the right edge of the callout supports a left-to-right reading that concludes with this link as an optional next step for users needing more insurance detail.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the insurance promotion card; flows with text and wraps on smaller screens, potentially moving beneath the main copy but maintaining a slight offset or icon to signal clickability.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This link is placed near the very bottom of the booking page, just below the booking-submit-controller, separated by about 16–24px. It is left-aligned within the central column, occupying only the width of its text content (a single line), and styled as a simple text link, perhaps with a small phone or chat icon, in a softer teal or gray to denote its secondary nature. Its location makes it an unobtrusive off-ramp for users who are unsure about booking online or who have questions before submitting. The generous white space beneath it and above any page footer keeps it distinct from the main call-to-action bar while still clearly associated with the booking process.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline/block text element in the flow; on narrow screens it remains full-width but visually lightweight, wrapping to multiple lines if necessary.

name: Emergency Contact Form
title: Emergency Contact Form
description: Page for patients to send an urgent emergency message to the practice, including condition details, time of the emergency, the emergency phone number being used, and callback request preferences.

core_features: Display an emergency contact form that captures condition type, description of the emergency, and the time the issue occurred.
Pre-populate relevant fields using incoming URL parameters for condition type, emergency phone number, and optional event time hint.
Require users to confirm or enter the after-hours emergency phone number they are using, and allow them to explicitly request a callback, including an option that represents a same-night callback.
Collect required callback details (contact name, phone, optional email, optional patient name) and submit an EmergencyMessage using the submitEmergencyMessage interface.
Show validation and submission status (in progress, success, or error) using the response from submitEmergencyMessage.
Provide navigation options after submission to return to the emergency instructions page (preserving condition type via URL parameters) or return to the homepage.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – After-hours emergency coming from Emergency Dental Care page: 1) User reads condition-specific instructions on emergency_care.html and clicks a link that opens emergency_form.html with URL parameters for conditionType, emergencyPhone, and optionally eventTimeHint. 2) On load, the form auto-fills the condition type, the emergency phone number field, and optionally hints the time of the emergency. 3) User reviews the pre-filled emergency phone number, describes their symptoms and context, and confirms or edits the time of the emergency. 4) User indicates whether they are requesting a callback, and if so selects a callback time preference, including an option that represents a same-night callback. 5) User enters callback details (contact name, phone, optional email, and optional patient name). 6) User submits the form, which validates required fields and then calls submitEmergencyMessage with the collected values. 7) On success, the page displays the confirmation message returned by submitEmergencyMessage and exposes links to go back to emergency instructions (with conditionType in the URL) or to the homepage.

Workflow 2 – Direct access to the Emergency Contact Form (no URL parameters): 1) User opens emergency_form.html directly without URL parameters. 2) On load, fields are empty; the user manually selects a condition type and enters the emergency phone number they are using, a description of the situation, and the time the problem occurred. 3) User indicates whether a callback is requested and selects a callback time preference if applicable. 4) User provides contact details. 5) User submits the form; submitEmergencyMessage is called with the form data. 6) The user sees submission status and, if successful, can navigate back to emergency_care.html (using the selected condition type) or to index.html.

Workflow 3 – Requesting a specific same-night callback: 1) User arrives on emergency_form.html (via emergency_care.html or directly) and confirms the emergency phone number they see or enters it manually. 2) User checks the option indicating they want a callback and selects a callback time preference option that represents a same-night callback. 3) User completes all mandatory fields and submits the form. 4) submitEmergencyMessage is called with callbackRequested set to true and callbackTimePreference set accordingly. 5) The success state shows the confirmation message and clearly indicates that a callback has been requested, and the user uses the navigation links as needed.
interactions: On page load, the form reads conditionType, emergencyPhone, and eventTimeHint from the URL query string and uses them to pre-fill corresponding fields (condition type selector or display, emergency phone number input, and event time input or description hint).

The user can change the conditionType field even if it was pre-populated, so that the message reflects their actual situation; this value is later sent in submitEmergencyMessage and reused when linking back to emergency_care.html.

The user must confirm or enter the emergency phone number in a dedicated input field; this value is used both for display on the page and as the emergencyPhoneNumberDisplayed parameter in submitEmergencyMessage.
The user enters a free-text description of symptoms and circumstances; this is bound to the description parameter for submitEmergencyMessage.
The user enters a human-readable time for when the emergency started or worsened; this is bound to eventTimeText for submitEmergencyMessage.
The user toggles whether a callback is requested; when enabled, the user selects a callbackTimePreference option (including an option representing a same-night callback) before submission.
The user enters contactName, contactPhone, optional contactEmail, and optional patientName; these are mapped directly to the corresponding parameters of submitEmergencyMessage.

When the user clicks the form submit button, the page validates required fields (including the presence of conditionType, description, eventTimeText, emergencyPhoneNumberDisplayed, contactName, and contactPhone, and callbackTimePreference when a callback is requested). If validation passes, submitEmergencyMessage is called with current form state.
While submitEmergencyMessage is in progress, the submit button is disabled and a processing state is shown.

After submitEmergencyMessage resolves with success, the page displays the confirmation_message and uses the status and emergencyMessageId to update the status panel; the form can be locked to prevent further edits.
If submitEmergencyMessage returns a non-success result or the call fails, an error state is shown and the user can correct the form and resubmit.
When the user clicks "Back to emergency instructions" in the confirmation area, the page navigates to emergency_care.html with the current conditionType encoded as a URL parameter.
When the user clicks "Return to homepage" in the confirmation area, the page navigates to index.html without additional parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by URL query parameters and transient in-memory form state, without using localStorage. On initialization, the URL is parsed for three parameters: conditionType, emergencyPhone, and eventTimeHint. If conditionType is present, it is used to initialize the condition type control and stored in page state; if it is absent, the condition type control starts empty and the user must select or enter it. If emergencyPhone is present, it is used to pre-fill the emergency phone number input and stored as the default value for emergencyPhoneNumberDisplayed; the user can edit this value before submission. If eventTimeHint is present, it is used to initialize the event time input and optionally appended or referenced in the description field as a hint; if absent, the event time input is empty. Internally, the page maintains a submissionStatus state (such as idle, submitting, success, or error), the latest API response data (success, emergencyMessageId, status, confirmation_message), and the current form values. No additional API calls or interfaces are used beyond submitEmergencyMessage. Navigation to emergency_care.html uses the current conditionType from page state in the query string (e.g., emergency_care.html?conditionType=<currentConditionType>), so that the instructions page can restore the appropriate emergency instructions. Navigation to index.html does not depend on any parameters. The page does not persist state across reloads except via the URL parameters originally provided or passed on via navigation.

components.functionality: On initial page load, this component parses the query string for conditionType, emergencyPhone, and eventTimeHint and maps them into the initial form state. It initializes the condition type field, the emergency phone number input, and the event time field or description hint. It ensures that these values are available to other components via shared page state.

Displays context about how the emergency contact form is used. It shows the emergency phone number from the emergency phone number field so users can confirm that they are using the correct number and understand that urgent issues should be called as well as sent via this form. It also explains how to indicate a callback request and highlights the option representing a same-night callback when the user wants to be contacted that night.

Collects all data required to send an emergency message and request a callback. It presents fields for condition type (initialized from URL if provided), emergency description, event time, emergency phone number confirmation, callback requested toggle, callback time preference selection, patient name, contact name, contact phone, and optional contact email. When submitted, it validates the inputs and, if valid, calls submitEmergencyMessage with the current form values. The result of submitEmergencyMessage is stored in page state and triggers updates in the status and navigation components.

Evaluates the current form state and displays validation feedback to the user. It checks for required values (conditionType, description, eventTimeText, emergency phone number, contactName, contactPhone, and callbackTimePreference when a callback is requested) and any basic formatting rules (such as phone and email formats as defined by the implementation). It surfaces field-level and form-level messages and prevents submission from proceeding until validation passes.

Reflects the current submission state of the emergency message. While submitEmergencyMessage is in progress, it shows a submitting state and can disable the form’s submit button. On successful completion, it displays the confirmation_message and may show the returned status and emergencyMessageId in a non-identifying way suitable for the user (for example, indicating that the message has been received and is being reviewed). On error, it shows an error state and allows the user to retry.

Provides navigation options after a successful submission. It renders a link or button to go back to emergency_care.html, appending the current conditionType from page state as a URL parameter, so that the corresponding emergency instructions can be shown again. It also renders a link or button to return to index.html without additional parameters. These links are enabled only when submissionStatus indicates a successful submission.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a max-width content container (~1140–1200px). The row of four circular service cards suggests four equal-width columns on desktop.
layout.layout_pattern: This page inherits the global header/hero/navigation and four circular service cards from the main site template. All emergency-contact-specific components sit in the central content column beneath that visual band. The arrangement is linear and top-to-bottom: after the page H1 and brief introductory copy (provided by the template), the callback instructions appear first as an info panel, followed by any global validation messages, then the main emergency contact form. After form interaction, a submission status panel and contextual navigation links appear beneath the form. Invisible state initialization runs in the background and does not affect spatial layout.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: This page inherits the global header/hero/navigation and four circular service cards from the main site template. All emergency-contact-specific components sit in the central content column beneath that visual band. The arrangement is linear and top-to-bottom: after the page H1 and brief introductory copy (provided by the template), the callback instructions appear first as an info panel, followed by any global validation messages, then the main emergency contact form. After form interaction, a submission status panel and contextual navigation links appear beneath the form. Invisible state initialization runs in the background and does not affect spatial layout.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content is centered within a max-width container of ~1140–1200px, with the active content column occupying about 60–70% of the viewport width on large screens (roughly 8 of 12 grid columns), centered with generous side margins. Vertical spacing between major blocks (instructions, validation summary, form, status, navigation links) follows the 32–48px rhythm. Each card-like component (info panel, form, status panel) uses internal padding around 24–32px, while smaller subtitles and labels use tighter 12–16px spacing. On smaller screens, all components expand to nearly full width with consistent edge padding aligned to the global grid.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page continues the moderately spacious, editorial feel: large headings, comfortable line spacing, and clear separation between functional blocks. The instructions panel is concise and well-padded, the form fields are grouped with enough breathing room to avoid feeling clinical or overwhelming, and validation/status messages are clearly separated bands rather than crowded inline notices. Error messaging is prominent but not noisy, using one consolidated area near the top of the form plus subtle field-level cues, in line with the ‘trustworthy, friendly’ medical aesthetic.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow continues the Z-pattern established by the global layout: after the user’s gaze moves through the header and circular cards, it lands on the centered H1 'Emergency Contact Form' and introductory text. From there, the eye moves downward to the centered callback instructions panel (a lightly emphasized band), then slightly down and left into any validation summary (if present), then into the main form fields. After submission, attention is drawn downward to the status panel (using the brand green or a soft success/error tone), and finally to the post-submit navigation links at the bottom. The left-aligned form labels within a centered column preserve a clear reading path while reinforcing a professional, medical feel.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the existing hero, navigation bar, and the row of four circular service cards, the page transitions into a calm, centered content column that fills roughly two-thirds of the screen width on desktop. At the top of this column, the global H1 'Emergency Contact Form' and a short, centered explanation set the context with generous white space above and below. Immediately below this text, a softly highlighted instructions panel spans the full width of the content column, framed by rounded corners, a light background tint, and comfortable padding, visually echoing the card-like shapes from the hero band. A small gap below it leads into a more compact validation summary area that appears only when needed, aligned with the same column width. Below that, the main emergency contact form sits as the dominant, card-like block: labels are left-aligned, fields are arranged in one or two columns depending on viewport width, and the primary submit button anchors the bottom-right of the form area. When the form has been submitted, a status panel appears beneath the form (or in the same vertical region if the form collapses), with a colored accent bar or icon indicating success or urgency. At the very bottom of the column, a set of post-submit navigation links offers next steps, arranged in a simple row or short vertical list with ample spacing. The overall impression is of a structured, reassuring medical page where each functional step—understanding instructions, completing the form, seeing the outcome, and choosing what to do next—is clearly separated but smoothly connected along a single, top-to-bottom visual path.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component runs invisibly and does not occupy any visual space. It is conceptually positioned at the very top of the main content region, just inside the central content container and above the visible callback instructions panel. It should not render any UI; instead, it initializes the form state (such as pre-filling fields or toggling emergency flags) before the visible components are drawn. In terms of layout, it sits in the document flow but with display set to none, ensuring that the vertical rhythm of the page begins directly with the H1 and the instructions panel, without any extra gaps or offsets.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-height element that participates only in the logical render order, not in the visual layout; no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The callback instructions panel sits near the top of the main content column, directly beneath the page H1 and any introductory sentence. It is centered within the max-width container, occupying roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on large screens (matching the content column, about 8/12 grid columns), with equal left and right margins. There is a generous vertical gap of approximately 32–40px between the H1 block and the top edge of this panel, reinforcing its role as the first actionable content area. The panel itself is a horizontally oriented card: full-width within the column, with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides and a subtle drop shadow or soft border in line with the site’s medical/corporate style. Text is left-aligned within the panel, but the panel as a whole remains centered on the page. On desktop, a small circular or rounded icon (e.g., a phone or alert symbol using the brand teal/green gradient) can appear near the top-left inside the panel, with the heading and instructions text to its right, reinforcing the circular visual language from the hero cards. The bottom of the panel sits about 24–32px above the next element (validation messages or the form), creating clear separation without feeling disconnected.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level component in normal document flow; scales responsively to full width of the content column and stacks naturally above the form elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The emergency contact form is the primary focal block in the central column and sits directly beneath the callback instructions panel and any global validation summary. There is a vertical gap of about 24–32px above the form’s top edge. The form extends the full width of the content column (again, roughly 60–70% of the viewport, centered, about 8/12 columns), with a card-like appearance: a white background, rounded corners, soft shadow, and 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. Inside, the layout uses the underlying 12-column grid: labels are left-aligned, and on desktop, shorter fields (e.g., emergency phone number, preferred callback method, time of emergency) can be laid out as two columns (each ~6/12) with 16–24px of horizontal gutter, while longer fields such as condition details span the full card width (12/12) and appear stacked vertically. Vertical spacing between form groups is around 16–24px, keeping the layout open but efficient. The primary submit button sits near the bottom-right inside the form card, with 16–24px of space above it and 8–16px below it to the card edge, and may be paired with a secondary text link (e.g., ‘Cancel’ or ‘Call direct’) aligned to the left or just under the button. On smaller screens, the form collapses to a single column, with all fields spanning the full width of the card and maintaining at least 16px of edge padding. The form aligns visually with the instructions panel above and the status panel below, creating one continuous vertical spine of interaction.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level component that fills the central content column; internally, fields may align to a responsive two-column grid on larger screens and stack to one column on smaller screens. No fixed positioning; the form scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The validation messages component occupies a compact but clear band between the callback instructions panel and the top of the form. When there are no errors, this band is not visible, and the form sits roughly 24–32px below the instructions panel. When validation issues occur, a bar or small card appears, aligned to the same central content column width (about 60–70% of the viewport), with text left-aligned and a notable but restrained accent color (e.g., a soft red or amber with a subtle border). Its internal padding is about 12–16px, making it tighter than the main cards but still legible and in tune with the overall spacing rhythm. This component can contain a short summary message (e.g., ‘Please correct the highlighted fields below’) and possibly a bullet list of errors. It sits about 16–24px below the instructions panel and 16px above the form, visually connecting the guidance above with the corrective action below. At the field level, this component coordinates with inline messages (e.g., small red text under specific fields) without disturbing the overall grid; the summary remains the primary error indicator in this horizontal band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears dynamically within normal document flow. When present, it pushes the form downward but does not overlay any content; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission status panel is positioned directly beneath the emergency contact form within the central content column, occupying the same width and alignment as the form and other panels (about 60–70% of the viewport, centered). In the default (unsubmitted) state, this area may either be absent or reserved as a small vertical gap, maintaining a predictable flow. After the user submits the form, the status panel appears with a card-like or banner-like style that visually mirrors the instructions panel but with stronger color cues: success uses the brand green/teal tones with a light gradient or colored left border; error states may use a softer red accent. The panel has 24px of padding and sits 24–32px below the bottom of the form card. If desired, the form itself can become slightly de-emphasized after a successful submission (e.g., lightly dimmed or collapsed), making the status panel feel like the new focal area in this vertical stack. Text within the panel is left-aligned, with a bolded status heading (e.g., ‘Message Sent’ or ‘Unable to Send’) and a short explanatory sentence or two beneath. This panel visually anchors the outcome of the interaction before the user moves on to the navigation links below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level component appearing in normal document flow after form submission. It does not overlay the form; instead, it pushes subsequent content downward and can expand or collapse as status changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The post-submit navigation links sit at the bottom of the main content column, directly beneath the submission status panel. They are aligned to the same central column width but are visually lighter than the card components: typically rendered as text links or small button-style links without heavy shadows. There is a vertical gap of about 24–32px between the status panel and the first navigation link. On desktop, if there are two or three key actions (e.g., ‘Return to Home Page’, ‘View Office Hours’, ‘Fill Out Another Form’), they can be arranged horizontally in a row aligned left within the column, with 16–24px horizontal spacing between them, or stacked as a short vertical list with 8–12px vertical spacing, depending on content length. The typography follows the site’s sans-serif UI style, with link colors using the brand teal/green and subtle hover states. On mobile, the links stack vertically and extend close to the full width of the content area, maintaining at least 16px side padding. This group of links acts as a gentle exit path from the form experience, clearly separated from the functional form and status blocks above but still visually part of the same vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow navigation group at the bottom of the page’s main content column, adapting from a horizontal layout on wider screens to a stacked layout on smaller screens.

name: Locations & Hours
title: Locations & Hours
description: Displays all clinic locations with addresses, contact details, and office hours, and lets users choose a location before booking or asking a question.

core_features: Load all clinic locations by calling getAllLocations and keep them in page state for display and filtering.
Display each location’s core details (name, address fields, city, state, postal_code, phone) using data from the Location model.

Render office hours for each location based on the Location.hours array, clearly indicating per-day open and close times and whether Saturday and evening hours are available using has_saturday_hours and has_evening_hours.
Use the optional highlightLocationId URL parameter to emphasize a specific location on page load (e.g., visually highlight and auto-expand its hours and scroll it into view if found).
Allow users to filter or quickly focus on locations that have Saturday hours and/or evening hours using the has_saturday_hours and has_evening_hours fields without additional API calls.
Provide a "Book at this location" action for each location that navigates to appointment_booking.html with URL parameters sourcePage=locations_page and locationId set to the selected location’s id.
Provide an "Ask about this location" action for each location that navigates to contact.html with URL parameters submissionType=general_question and subject=Location or hours question.
If latitude and longitude are present for a location, provide a functional "View on map" link or button that opens an external map service for that location.
Handle cases where a location has no hours data (Location.hours missing or empty) by indicating that hours information is not available and directing users to call the location phone number.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Choose a location and start booking
1. User navigates to locations.html (optionally with ?highlightLocationId={locationId}).
2. Page controller calls getAllLocations and stores the returned list of Location entities.
3. Locations list displays all locations with name, address, phone, and a summary of hours.
4. If highlightLocationId is present and matches a Location.id, that location card is emphasized and its full hours are expanded.
5. User scans the list and selects a preferred location.
6. User clicks "Book at this location" within the chosen location card.
7. Browser navigates to appointment_booking.html with URL parameters sourcePage=locations_page and locationId set to the selected Location.id so the booking page can pre-select that location.

Workflow: Find a location with Saturday or evening hours
1. User opens locations.html; getAllLocations loads all Location entities.
2. The filter controls are initialized with default values (no filters active).
3. User toggles the Saturday-hours filter control; the page filters the in-memory list to only locations where has_saturday_hours is true.
4. Optionally, user also toggles the evening-hours filter to further restrict to locations where has_evening_hours is true.
5. Filtered locations are displayed; if highlightLocationId is set but filtered out, the highlight is cleared or indicated as not currently visible.
6. User expands a location’s hours to verify specific days/times.
7. User chooses either "Book at this location" to proceed to appointment_booking.html or "Ask about this location" to proceed to contact.html with the prescribed URL parameters.

Workflow: Follow a highlighted location from another page
1. User clicks a link on index.html or dentist_profile.html that points to locations.html with ?highlightLocationId={locationId}.
2. On load, the page controller parses highlightLocationId from the URL.
3. The page calls getAllLocations and receives the list of Location entities.
4. Once locations are loaded, the controller finds the location whose id matches highlightLocationId.
5. If found, the page marks this location as highlighted, auto-expands its full hours details, and scrolls the page to its card.
6. User reviews this specific location’s address, hours, and contact information.
7. User can then either click "Book at this location" to go directly to appointment_booking.html with the location preselected, or click "Ask about this location" to go to contact.html with the general question subject populated.
interactions: On page load, the locations-page-controller reads the highlightLocationId query parameter (if present), calls getAllLocations, and updates page state with the list of locations and the id to highlight.

User can toggle a Saturday-hours filter control (e.g., a checkbox or switch). When toggled, the filter state is updated and the in-memory list of locations is re-filtered where has_saturday_hours is true or false as appropriate.
User can toggle an evening-hours filter control. When toggled, locations are filtered by has_evening_hours without any additional API calls.

User can click a location card header or "View details" control to expand or collapse that location’s detailed hours section. The expandedLocationId state is updated so that only one (or multiple, depending on design) locations are expanded.

When a user clicks "Book at this location" on a location card, a navigation event is triggered that changes the browser location to appointment_booking.html with query parameters sourcePage=locations_page and locationId set to that Location.id.

When a user clicks "Ask about this location" on a location card, a navigation event is triggered that changes the browser location to contact.html with query parameters submissionType=general_question and subject=Location or hours question.

If a location has latitude and longitude, a "View on map" link or button is shown; clicking it opens a new browser tab/window with an external mapping URL constructed using that location’s coordinates.

If highlightLocationId is not present initially but the user clicks on a location card to focus it (e.g., expand details), the page may update the URL query string to include highlightLocationId for deep-linking, without reloading the page.
If highlightLocationId refers to a non-existent Location.id (not found in getAllLocations results), the page ignores the highlight state and displays all locations normally.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary external state is managed via URL query parameters and synchronized with internal page state. The highlightLocationId query parameter is optional and is read on initial load and on URL changes. On initialization, the page parses highlightLocationId from window.location, stores it as highlightedLocationId in page state, then calls getAllLocations. After getAllLocations resolves, the controller searches the locations array for a Location whose id matches highlightedLocationId. If found, the component marks this location as highlighted (used for styling/emphasis), sets expandedLocationId to the same id so that its details and full hours are visible by default, and scrolls that card into view. If no matching location is found, highlightedLocationId and expandedLocationId are left unset.

Filter state for Saturday and evening availability is held in memory (e.g., hasSaturdayFilter, hasEveningFilter) and is not persisted via separate storage but may optionally be reflected in the URL as additional query parameters (e.g., hasSaturday=true, hasEvening=true). When these filters change, the controller recomputes a derived filteredLocations array from the original locations.
