If filters are added such that the currently highlightedLocationId is no longer present in filteredLocations, the page either clears the highlight or leaves it noted as "filtered out" depending on design; in either case, no additional API calls are made.

Whenever the user changes focus to a specific location (for example, by expanding its details), the controller may update the URL’s highlightLocationId parameter using the browser history API to allow deep linking to that location without a full page reload. All navigation from this page to other pages is done via URL query parameters only, using the specified formats: appointment_booking.html?sourcePage=locations_page&locationId={Location.id} for booking, and contact.html?submissionType=general_question&subject=Location%20or%20hours%20question for questions.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading via getAllLocations, processes URL parameters (notably highlightLocationId), manages filter and selection state, and passes derived data (such as filtered and highlighted locations) to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Parse highlightLocationId from the URL, call getAllLocations, and initialize state including highlightedLocationId, expandedLocationId, and filter flags.

onLocationsLoaded: Receive the array returned by getAllLocations, store it as locations in state, compute filteredLocations based on active filters, and if highlightedLocationId is set, find and mark the matching location for emphasis and expansion.

onFilterChange: Triggered when child filter controls change; updates filter flags (e.g., hasSaturdayFilter, hasEveningFilter), recomputes filteredLocations from locations, and reconciles highlight/expanded state.
onLocationExpand: Triggered when a user expands a location’s details; sets expandedLocationId and optionally updates highlightLocationId to match, also updating the URL query string accordingly.

onURLChange: Listens for browser history changes (e.g., back/forward navigation), re-parses highlightLocationId, and updates highlightedLocationId and expandedLocationId without re-calling getAllLocations unless the page is reloaded.

components.functionality: Provides interactive controls to narrow down the list of locations based on availability characteristics such as Saturday hours and evening hours, without issuing additional API calls.
event_handlers: onSaturdayFilterToggle: When the user toggles the Saturday filter control, updates a hasSaturdayFilter flag and notifies the page controller to recompute filtered locations.
onEveningFilterToggle: When the user toggles the evening filter control, updates a hasEveningFilter flag and notifies the page controller to recompute filtered locations.

onFiltersSyncFromURL: When initialized, reads any filter-related query parameters (if implemented, such as hasSaturday or hasEvening) and sets the filter control states to match, then emits a filter-change event to the controller.

components.functionality: Renders a list of location cards based on the filteredLocations data from the controller. Each card shows the location’s name, address, phone, summary of hours, and primary actions (book, ask, map). It also visually indicates which location is highlighted and manages expand/collapse of detailed hours via delegated events.
event_handlers: onLocationCardClick: When a user clicks on a location card header or a dedicated expand control, emits an event to the page controller with the selected Location.id so the controller can update expandedLocationId and highlightedLocationId.

onBookClick: When a user clicks "Book at this location" on a card, constructs a URL to appointment_booking.html with sourcePage=locations_page and locationId set to that Location.id, then triggers navigation.

onAskClick: When a user clicks "Ask about this location" on a card, constructs a URL to contact.html with submissionType=general_question and subject=Location or hours question, then triggers navigation.
onMapClick: When a user clicks "View on map" for a location that has latitude and longitude, constructs an external map URL based on those coordinates and opens it in a new tab or window.

onRenderHighlight: When rendering, checks each Location.id against highlightedLocationId supplied by the controller and applies a highlighted state (for emphasis and optional auto-expansion of hours).

components.functionality: Given a single Location entity, transforms its hours array into a per-day schedule, clearly formatting open_time and close_time pairs, and indicating days without hours. It also adds metadata such as whether the location offers Saturday or evening hours, based on has_saturday_hours and has_evening_hours.
event_handlers: onToggleVisibility: Tied to the expand/collapse control on the parent location card; when toggled, shows or hides the hours table for that location and notifies the controller via onLocationExpand.
onHoursDataChange: Reacts when the bound Location.hours value changes or is initially bound (after getAllLocations), regenerating the displayed schedule without additional API calls.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a max-width content container (~1140–1200px). The row of four circular service cards suggests four equal-width columns on desktop.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing global header/hero band and the row of four circular service cards, the Locations & Hours page content starts as a centered single column, mirroring the mockup’s pattern. The page-controller sits first as the main page-level selector and framing block, followed by a horizontal filter bar spanning the central column. Beneath the filter bar, the layout transitions into a two-column arrangement inside the same central content container: the location list on the left and the hours/detail table on the right, all aligned within the 12-column grid and max-width container.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing global header/hero band and the row of four circular service cards, the Locations & Hours page content starts as a centered single column, mirroring the mockup’s pattern. The page-controller sits first as the main page-level selector and framing block, followed by a horizontal filter bar spanning the central column. Beneath the filter bar, the layout transitions into a two-column arrangement inside the same central content container: the location list on the left and the hours/detail table on the right, all aligned within the 12-column grid and max-width container.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header + nav + circular hero cards still occupy the top ~45–50% of the initial viewport. The Locations & Hours main title and page-controller area take another ~15–20% with generous vertical breathing room. The filter bar is compact but clearly separated (~64px tall including padding and margins). The remaining vertical space (and scrolling depth) is dedicated to the split content zone where the location list uses roughly 40–45% of the central container width and the hours table uses 55–60%. Horizontal padding keeps all content within a ~1140–1200px max-width center-aligned container, with 32–48px vertical spacing between these major blocks.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content remains moderately spacious, echoing the mockup’s editorial feel. The page-controller and filter bar each get clear white space above and below (24–32px) so they read as distinct tiers. The location list presents locations as separated rows/cards with comfortable vertical gaps (16–24px). The hours table is formatted as a clean, legible table or detail card with ample cell padding, prioritizing quick scan of days and times over compactness. There is no tight, dashboard-style density; instead, the layout encourages relaxed scanning and easy touch targets.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The global page keeps the mockup’s Z-pattern in the header/hero: logo at top-left to CTAs top-right, down to the center row of circular cards, then down into the main content. Within the Locations & Hours section, the eye first lands on the centrally aligned page-controller (title and main selector), then moves slightly down to the horizontally oriented filter bar. From there, the flow shifts into an F-shaped scan: left column location list (top to bottom) then across to the right column hours/details for the selected location. The teal/green accents on interactive elements and headings act as anchors that guide the user step-by-step from choosing a location to understanding its hours.

layout.overall_description: After the familiar hero band with logo, nav, and the full-width row of four circular feature cards, the page scrolls into a calm, centered content area dedicated to Locations & Hours. A wide, central block introduces the page with a serif H1 and brief subheading, wrapped into the locations-page-controller and visually breathing with generous top and bottom margins. Directly beneath, a horizontal filter bar stretches across the same central column, framed like a subtle card with a light background and soft shadow, containing search and filter controls that echo the site’s teal brand color. Below this, the content splits into two columns inside the same max-width container: on the left, a vertical stack of location items with clear separation and clickable rows; on the right, a slightly more dominant detail panel that presents the selected location’s hours and key contact details in a tidy, table-like card. The two columns share a top baseline and maintain balanced whitespace around them, creating a structured but approachable layout that feels consistent with a traditional, trustworthy medical practice site. As users scroll, they move from broad page-level choice to focused location selection and finally to precise hours information, all while staying within the familiar centered grid and spacious rhythm established in the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The locations-page-controller is the first element of the Locations & Hours content area, positioned directly below the hero band and circular cards with a generous top margin of about 48px (6 x 8px). It is centered horizontally within the main content container, which itself is centered on the page and constrained to approximately 1140–1200px width. The controller spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on large screens, leaving even side margins within the container to maintain the airy feel. Visually, this block includes the serif H1 for the page (e.g., “Locations & Hours”) centered at the top, a single-line or short-paragraph welcome or explanatory text beneath it, and immediately below that a prominent location-selection control (e.g., dropdown, segmented buttons, or simple tabs) also centered. Vertical spacing follows the mockup’s generous rhythm: ~16–24px between the H1 and subheading, and 24–32px between the subheading and the location selector. The entire controller block occupies roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height, creating a clear, calm introduction before the more functional filtering and list content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll; it scrolls with the page as normal, acting as the top anchor for the Locations & Hours section without any fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The location-filter-bar sits immediately below the locations-page-controller, separated by a vertical gap of about 24–32px to make it feel like the next interactive tier. It aligns to the same central content container and spans nearly the full width of that container (around 90–100% of the inner width), appearing as a horizontal band or card. The bar has a light, slightly elevated background (e.g., white or a very pale tinted panel) with rounded corners and a soft drop shadow consistent with the mockup’s circular cards and button treatments. Internally, controls are arranged left-to-right: typically a text search field aligned to the left, followed by pill or button-style filters spaced out with 16px gaps, and potentially an “Open now” toggle or distance filter toward the right. The vertical height of the bar, including padding, is around 56–64px, keeping it substantial enough to feel tappable while not overwhelming the screen. There is 24px of whitespace below the bar before the two-column content begins, clearly separating it from the list and hours table. On narrower screens, the filter controls wrap to multiple lines but still remain grouped within this same framed bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, the filter bar can optionally become sticky at the top of the main content area once scrolled into position, staying pinned just below the global nav/hero threshold so users can adjust filters while browsing the location list. On mobile, it scrolls normally to avoid consuming too much vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The location-list begins directly beneath the filter bar, sharing a top baseline with the location-hours-table to its right. Together they form a two-column layout inside the centered container, following the same implicit 12-column grid. The location-list occupies approximately 4–5 of the 12 columns, equating to roughly 40–45% of the container width on desktop. It is aligned to the left edge of the container, with the first list item’s title aligned vertically just below the 24px gap that separates the content from the filter bar. Each location in the list is presented as a horizontal card or row with a white background, subtle shadow, and 16–24px internal padding. Rows are stacked vertically with 16–24px gaps between them, maintaining the moderately spacious density of the mockup. Key information (location name, city, address snippet, and primary phone) is left-aligned within each row, with a small teal accent (e.g., a circular bullet or left border) indicating the currently selected location. The list area has enough vertical height to show 3–6 locations on common laptop screens before scrolling, roughly 40–50% of the viewport height under the filter bar. On taller screens, the list grows with content, while on smaller devices the two-column layout collapses: the location-list moves above the hours table to maintain a clear top-to-bottom selection flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The list scrolls with the page as a standard content column. On larger screens, it does not independently scroll within a fixed-height pane, in order to keep the layout feeling simple and in line with the traditional, non-dashboard aesthetic; instead, the entire page scroll carries both the list and the hours table.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The location-hours-table is positioned to the right of the location-list, starting at the same vertical line just below the filter bar, creating a balanced two-column layout. It occupies approximately 7–8 of the 12 columns, or about 55–60% of the container width, making it slightly more visually dominant than the location list to emphasize detailed hours information. The table sits with a 24–32px gap to the left column, providing comfortable separation while clearly grouping the two as related content. Visually, this component appears as a single, elevated card with a white background, subtle drop shadow, and rounded corners. At the top of the card, the selected location’s name and full address are left-aligned, with the name in a slightly larger, bold serif or strong sans-serif to echo heading styles. Below, the hours are presented in a clean, grid-like table: days of the week in a left column, hours in a right column, with each row having ample vertical padding (~12–16px) for easy scanning. If additional details are included (holiday schedule, lunch breaks, emergency instructions), they appear below the main hours grid with 16–24px spacing. The height of this card roughly matches or slightly exceeds the height of the visible location list on initial load, giving a stable side-by-side look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, the hours table can be configured to stick within its column as the user scrolls down the list (a sticky position starting a little below the nav/hero), so the selected location’s hours remain visible while browsing other locations. On smaller viewports, this sticky behavior is disabled, and the component stacks below the location-list in a single column, maintaining a linear scroll experience.

name: About Our Practice
title: About Our Practice
description: Provides dynamically loaded information about the dental practice’s mission, history, care for adults and children, specialties, technology, and safety, and offers navigation to related pages such as the dentist list and new patient information.

core_features: Load and display the practice mission, history, adult care summary, child care summary, specialties, and technology/safety information using the getPracticeOverview interface.
Provide in-page section navigation (e.g., Mission, History, Adult Care, Child Care, Specialties, Technology & Safety) driven by URL parameters so users can deep-link to specific sections.
Allow users to focus content on adult or child care via URL parameters and in-page toggles, adjusting which section is emphasized on load.
Display a dynamically generated specialties list from getPracticeOverview.specialties_highlight with per-specialty details and actions.

Provide context-aware navigation links to dentist_list.html (e.g., from the specialties area) and new_patients.html (e.g., from adult/child care sections), passing relevant context through URL parameters.
Handle loading and error states for the getPracticeOverview call, with retry capability, without hardcoding any practice-specific data.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – View general information about the practice: (1) User opens about.html (optionally with URL parameters). (2) The page calls getPracticeOverview and displays a loading state. (3) When data is returned, the Mission section is shown by default (or another section based on URL parameters). (4) User reads through the sections and scrolls as needed using the section navigation.

Workflow 2 – Jump directly to adult or child care information: (1) User arrives at about.html with a URL parameter like ?patient=adult or ?patient=child (from another page or a saved link). (2) The route controller derives the active section (e.g., Adult Care or Child Care) from the patient parameter and updates the section parameter if needed. (3) The corresponding section is scrolled into view and marked active in the navigation. (4) User reads the summary and, if desired, uses the provided link to go to new_patients.html with the same patient context.

Workflow 3 – Explore specialties and proceed to meet dentists: (1) User opens about.html and navigates to or clicks the Specialties section tab. (2) The specialties list is populated from getPracticeOverview.specialties_highlight. (3) User clicks on a specific specialty item, which updates the URL parameter (e.g., ?section=specialties&specialty={specialty}) and marks that specialty as active. (4) Within the selected specialty item, user clicks a link to meet dentists for that specialty, which opens dentist_list.html with appropriate URL parameters (e.g., dentist_list.html?from=about&specialty={specialty}).

Workflow 4 – View technology and safety information: (1) User navigates to about.html and selects the Technology & Safety section from the in-page navigation. (2) The route controller updates the section URL parameter. (3) The page scrolls or jumps to the Technology & Safety section, which renders getPracticeOverview.technology_and_safety. (4) User reads the information and can then use section navigation to move to other sections.

Workflow 5 – Recover from data load errors: (1) User opens about.html. (2) getPracticeOverview fails (e.g., network error). (3) The data provider shows an error state with a retry action; content sections are disabled or show a generic error indicator. (4) User clicks Retry, which re-invokes getPracticeOverview. (5) On success, sections become interactive and display the loaded content.
interactions: Initial data load: On page initialization, the practice-overview-data-provider component calls getPracticeOverview with no parameters, sets a loading flag, and then exposes the returned mission, history, adult_care_summary, child_care_summary, specialties_highlight, and technology_and_safety to all bound content components.

Section selection via navigation: When a user clicks a section tab (e.g., Mission, History, Adult Care, Child Care, Specialties, Technology & Safety), the section-navigation component triggers an onClickTab handler that updates the section URL parameter (using the route controller) and adjusts visible/active content accordingly.

Adult/Child emphasis toggle: If the user clicks an Adult Care or Child Care emphasis control (e.g., toggle or buttons), an onClick handler updates the patient URL parameter (e.g., patient=adult or patient=child) and, if necessary, the section parameter (e.g., section=adult-care or section=child-care), then scrolls the corresponding section into view.

Specialty selection: When a user clicks on a specialty item within the specialties-list component, an onClickSpecialty handler marks that specialty as active, updates the specialty URL parameter, and ensures that the associated description is expanded or highlighted while other specialties remain available.

Navigate to dentist list from About page: Within the specialties-list or another appropriate area, when a user clicks a “Meet our dentists” type link, an onClickDentistLink handler computes a target URL of dentist_list.html with appended parameters (e.g., from=about and optionally specialty={activeSpecialty}) and navigates the browser to that URL.

Navigate to new patient information from About page: Within the adult-care-section or child-care-section, when a user clicks an “Information for new patients” type link, an onClickNewPatientLink handler navigates to new_patients.html with parameters (e.g., from=about and patient={derivedPatientType}).
Retry on data load error: If getPracticeOverview fails, an onRetry handler in the error-display component re-invokes getPracticeOverview and resets the loading and error states accordingly.

URL-driven state changes: When the URL’s query string changes (e.g., via back/forward navigation or direct edits), the route-controller component listens to the popstate event and updates internal state (activeSection, patientType, activeSpecialty), which triggers the relevant content components to update their displayed state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is derived primarily from the result of getPracticeOverview and from URL query parameters. Query parameters are used instead of localStorage and are the single source of truth for view-related state. Suggested parameters: (1) section: string identifying the active section of the page. Expected values include mission, history, adult-care, child-care, specialties, technology-safety. If section is missing or does not match a known section, a default (e.g., mission or a value inferred from patient) is applied and may be written back to the URL. (2) patient: string indicating the primary patient perspective for the visit, such as adult or child. This parameter influences which section is emphasized on initial load. For example, if patient=child, the route-controller may set section=child-care if section is not explicitly provided. (3) specialty: string representing the currently focused specialty item in the specialties list; this value should match the specialty identifier returned in getPracticeOverview.specialties_highlight[i].specialty when present. If specialty is provided and matches an item, that item is considered active (expanded/highlighted). If it does not match, no specialty is treated as active. On page initialization, the route-controller reads the current URL parameters and computes derived state: activeSection (based on section and possibly patient), patientType (based on patient or undefined), and activeSpecialty (based on specialty). These derived values are shared via a central page state object. When the user interacts with navigation, toggles, or specialty items, the corresponding handlers update the URL parameters using the History API (e.g., pushState or replaceState) and recompute activeSection, patientType, and activeSpecialty. Content components are pure consumers of this state and of the PracticeOverview data from getPracticeOverview. No static or hardcoded practice content is stored in the page state; all descriptive text is bound directly to fields returned from getPracticeOverview.

components.functionality: Fetches the practice overview data using the getPracticeOverview interface on page initialization, manages loading and error states, and exposes the resulting PracticeOverview object (including mission, history, adult_care_summary, child_care_summary, specialties_highlight, and technology_and_safety) to other components via a shared state or context.

Reads and writes URL query parameters (section, patient, specialty) to control which section of the About page is active, which patient type (adult or child) is emphasized, and which specialty is focused. It listens for browser navigation events to keep the page state in sync with the URL and notifies content and navigation components of state changes.

Renders clickable controls for each logical section of the About page (e.g., Mission, History, Adult Care, Child Care, Specialties, Technology & Safety). Highlights the currently active section based on RouteState.activeSection and triggers updates to the section URL parameter when a user selects a different section.

Provides controls that allow users to emphasize content for adults or children (e.g., buttons or a toggle). When a user selects a patient type, it updates the patient URL parameter and may change the active section (e.g., to Adult Care or Child Care) via the route-controller.

Displays the mission text of the practice when the active section is mission. It is a read-only view bound to PracticeOverview.mission and only renders its content when RouteState.activeSection indicates that the mission section should be visible.
Displays the history of the practice when the active section is history. It binds directly to PracticeOverview.history and is shown or hidden based on RouteState.activeSection.

Shows the adult care summary content when the active section is adult-care. It binds to PracticeOverview.adult_care_summary and can provide a contextual link to the new patient information page, including any relevant URL parameters derived from RouteState.patientType.

Shows the child care summary content when the active section is child-care. It binds to PracticeOverview.child_care_summary and can offer a contextual link to new_patients.html, including a patient parameter indicating child-focused content.

Renders a list of specialties based on PracticeOverview.specialties_highlight. For each specialty item, it displays the provided description and supports selecting a specialty, which updates RouteState.activeSpecialty and the specialty URL parameter. It can provide a contextual link to dentist_list.html, passing the selected specialty via query parameters.

Displays information about the practice’s technology and safety protocols when the active section is technology-safety. It binds directly to PracticeOverview.technology_and_safety and is controlled by RouteState.activeSection.

Provides generic navigational links from the About page to dentist_list.html and new_patients.html. It composes destination URLs with context parameters (such as from=about, patient, and specialty) based on RouteState and user interactions, without loading any additional data.

Shows loading indicators while getPracticeOverview is in progress and displays error messaging or retry controls if the call fails. It hides or disables dependent content sections when data is not available.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a max-width content container (~1140–1200px). The row of four circular service cards suggests four equal-width columns on desktop.
layout.layout_pattern: All About-page components sit within the existing global frame (hero image, logo, top-right CTA cluster, full-width teal main nav, and the row of four circular cards). The About content begins immediately below the circular cards as a single centered column constrained to the ~1140–1200px grid (roughly 60–70% of a large desktop viewport). At the top of this column, the route controller renders the page-level H1 and introduction, followed by a local horizontal section-navigation, then the patient-focus toggle and any status messaging. Beneath that, the content flows linearly in stacked sections: mission, history, adult care, child care, specialties, technology & safety, and finally cross-page links.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All About-page components sit within the existing global frame (hero image, logo, top-right CTA cluster, full-width teal main nav, and the row of four circular cards). The About content begins immediately below the circular cards as a single centered column constrained to the ~1140–1200px grid (roughly 60–70% of a large desktop viewport). At the top of this column, the route controller renders the page-level H1 and introduction, followed by a local horizontal section-navigation, then the patient-focus toggle and any status messaging. Beneath that, the content flows linearly in stacked sections: mission, history, adult care, child care, specialties, technology & safety, and finally cross-page links.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header/hero and circular cards occupy the upper 40–50% of the first viewport, with the About content taking over immediately below. Within the About area, each major section is treated as its own vertical block with generous top and bottom spacing (typically 32–48px between sections). All text content stays within the central column, while select feature bands (like technology & safety) can extend their background color full-width but keep their content aligned to the same centered container. No component exceeds the central column width; internal elements (like lists) may split into 2–3 subcolumns inside that width on desktop.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate and editorial: prominent headings, short paragraphs, clear section breaks, and selective use of multi-column lists. Vertical whitespace is generous around headings and between sections, and tappable/interactive elements like the section-navigation tabs and patient-focus toggle have comfortable padding (around 12–20px). Data-heavy areas like the specialties list are broken into spacious multi-column layouts to prevent visual crowding, maintaining the approachable, traditional medical feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The page continues the Z-pattern established by the header: logo left to CTAs right, down to the circular cards band, then down into the centered About column. Within the About area, the flow becomes more of an F-pattern: eyes catch the page H1, drop to the section-navigation bar, then scan down the left-aligned section headings (mission, history, etc.) and into the body text. The sticky section-navigation (local teal/underlined links) acts as a horizontal anchor, while the technology & safety full-width band and the cross-page links block near the bottom provide strong visual waypoints that break up the vertical scroll and guide users to actions.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the hero, main navigation, and the row of four circular feature circles, the About Our Practice page transitions into a calm, centered content column. At the top of this column, the page title and a brief introductory line sit with generous breathing room, echoing the editorial feel of the mockup. Directly under this intro, a low-profile horizontal section-navigation bar spans the central column, providing anchor links to Mission, History, Adult Care, Child Care, Specialties, and Technology & Safety. Just below, a compact patient-focus toggle sits toward the upper right of the column, letting visitors bias the page toward adult or child information. As users scroll, they move down through clearly separated sections: an inviting mission statement, a lightly accented history band, paired adult and child care sections, a multi-column specialties list, and a prominent, tinted technology & safety band that stretches edge-to-edge while keeping content centered. The page ends with a tidy cross-page links block, centered and slightly more compact, pointing users toward dentist profiles and new patient resources. Throughout, spacing is generous, alignment is consistent with the 12-column grid, and the teal/green accents, soft shadows, and traditional typography maintain the trustworthy, slightly classic medical aesthetic of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is a non-visual data wrapper that conceptually occupies the full About-page content area but renders no UI. It sits directly below the global circular cards band in the hierarchy and wraps the entire central content column (including the route controller, navigation, and all sections). It adopts the same centered max-width container (~1140–1200px) as the rest of the content but does not introduce any extra padding or margins; visually, users see only the components it enables.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible structural wrapper; spans the full scrollable About content column without affecting layout, spacing, or scroll behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The route controller sits at the top of the About page content column, just inside the data provider. It determines which of the About-related components are rendered and in what order. Visually, it manifests as the page-level structure: immediately below the global circular cards band, it introduces the centered H1 (e.g., “About Our Practice”) and optional short subheading, both aligned to the central column (about 60–70% of viewport width) with generous top and bottom spacing (~32–48px). Below this H1 block, it places the section-navigation, then the patient-focus toggle and status indicator, followed by the sequence of content sections (mission, history, adult care, child care, specialties, technology & safety, and finally the cross-page links block).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller that defines the vertical stacking order of the visible components; it scrolls naturally with the page and does not introduce fixed or sticky behavior itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-navigation appears as a low, horizontal band at the upper part of the About content column, immediately below the page H1/subheading block and just above the patient-focus toggle. It is centered within the same max-width container (~1140–1200px) and spans the full width of that column (approximately 60–70% of the viewport on desktop), with 16–20px vertical padding and subtle bottom border or shadow to echo the main teal nav bar above. Navigation items (Mission, History, Adult Care, Child Care, Specialties, Technology & Safety) are arranged left to right in a single row, left-aligned on desktop with 16–24px horizontal spacing between items. The band’s vertical height is modest (about 1/8 of the visible About content area above the fold), but its teal-accented links and subtle divider line make it stand out as the local anchor for intra-page navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky element within the content column: once the user scrolls past its original position, it remains fixed to the top of the viewport (within the page’s main content area) so that the section tabs remain accessible while the user scrolls through the lower sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The patient-focus-toggle is positioned directly beneath the section-navigation, aligned to the right edge of the central content column. Horizontally, it occupies roughly 20–30% of the column width, leaving comfortable whitespace to its left. Vertically, it sits in a compact strip with about 16px of space above and 24–32px of space below before the Mission section begins. Visually, it appears as a pill-shaped toggle with two or three options (e.g., ‘Adults’ and ‘Children’), using brand teal/green for the active side and a light neutral background for the inactive side. Despite its compact footprint, the control uses generous internal padding (about 10–14px vertically and 16–20px horizontally per option) to remain tappable and consistent with the mockup’s UI component sizing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow; it scrolls with the content and remains just above the first main content section. On smaller screens, it can stack underneath the section-navigation and center-align while retaining similar vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mission-section is the first substantial content block in the About column, appearing directly below the patient-focus toggle. It occupies the full width of the central column (aligned to the same ~1140–1200px max-width container) and is separated from the toggle by approximately 32px of vertical space. The section opens with a prominent heading (e.g., ‘Our Mission’) that is centered or slightly left-of-center, with 16–24px of space above the first line of body text. The body copy is arranged in a single, readable column that uses roughly 70–80% of the content width and is centered within the section, creating generous side margins. Top and bottom padding within this section are ample (about 32–48px), making the mission feel like a self-contained, editorial block. The background remains white, ensuring this section feels light and welcoming and setting a neutral baseline for the more accented sections below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section in the vertical flow; it does not stick or overlay other elements and simply scrolls upward as the user progresses down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The history-section follows directly beneath the mission-section, separated by around 32–40px of vertical space to clearly distinguish it as a new topic. It spans the same central column width and is visually treated as a slightly highlighted band: the background is a very light neutral or faint teal-tinted grey, extending the full width of the content column (not full-bleed across the viewport) to subtly differentiate it from the white mission section above. Inside, a left-aligned heading (‘Our History’) sits near the top of the band with about 24px of padding above it. The text below may be a mix of short paragraphs and a simple vertical timeline or date markers, but all remain within one column that is about 70–80% of the content width, maintaining generous side margins. Vertical padding inside this section is slightly more than average (approx. 40–48px top and bottom) to give the historical narrative a more spacious, storytelling feel while keeping overall density moderate.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content band; scrolls with the page and maintains the same horizontal alignment as other sections without special sticking or overlay behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The adult-care-section appears beneath the history-section, with approximately 40–48px of vertical separation. It spans the full width of the central content column and internally uses a soft two-column layout to communicate services and approach for adults. On desktop, the left 55–60% of the section is dedicated to text (heading plus paragraphs describing adult care), while the right 40–45% hosts supporting content such as key bullet points, a short list of adult-focused services, or a representative icon/illustration. Both columns are aligned to the underlying 12-column grid. The section’s heading is left-aligned at the top, with 16–24px of space before the body text begins. Vertical padding top and bottom is around 32–40px, making the section roughly 1/3 of a typical viewport height on desktop. The background remains white to keep continuity with the mission section, but subtle dividers or lightly tinted callout boxes can be used within the right column to break up information while maintaining the moderately spacious density.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that scrolls naturally; on smaller screens, the two columns collapse into a single column with the text appearing above the supporting bullet list or illustration.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The child-care-section sits immediately below the adult-care-section with 32–40px of vertical separation, mirroring its structure but with a slightly warmer, more family-oriented tone. It uses the same central column width and roughly the same two-column distribution: left 55–60% for descriptive text about pediatric care, right 40–45% for highlights such as comfort measures, kid-friendly amenities, or age-specific services. To subtly differentiate it from the adult section, the background may use a very light, soft teal or off-white tint across the entire section while the interior content remains on white cards or panels with gentle shadows. The section heading is left-aligned at the top of the text column, with 16–24px spacing before body text, and the overall vertical padding is similar to the adult-care-section (about 32–40px). In combination with the patient-focus toggle above, this section helps users quickly visually locate adult vs. child content as they scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section; uses responsive behavior similar to the adult-care-section, collapsing into a single column on smaller viewports while maintaining its vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The specialties-list is positioned beneath the child-care-section with approximately 40–48px of vertical separation, signaling a transition from narrative content to more scannable information. It occupies the full central column width and opens with a left-aligned or centered heading (‘Our Specialties’) with about 16–20px of space before the list items. Below the heading, the specialties are arranged in a grid-like layout within the column: on desktop, two or three vertical columns of items (depending on screen width) with generous horizontal and vertical spacing (around 16–24px between items) to avoid visual clutter. Each specialty may appear as a single-line text row or a pill-style label; in either case, the items collectively fill about 70–80% of the content width, centered within the section with generous side margins. Vertical padding at the top and bottom of the section is around 32–40px, making the block feel compact yet breathable within the overall page flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static list section; adopts a responsive grid that collapses to a single column on smaller screens while preserving consistent margins and item spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The technology-safety-section is a major visual feature band that follows the specialties-list, separated by approximately 48–64px of vertical space to mark it as a distinct, high-importance area. Unlike previous sections, its background extends full-bleed across the viewport in a very light teal or safety-associated tint, creating a horizontal stripe that visually anchors the lower part of the page. Within this full-width band, the content remains constrained to the same centered ~1140–1200px grid. Inside the container, the section may be split into two equal halves: the left 50% focusing on modern technology (digital X-rays, intraoral cameras, etc.) and the right 50% on safety protocols (sterilization procedures, infection control, patient safety policies). Each half has its own subsection heading and bullet points, with 24–32px of vertical padding above the content. Overall vertical padding within the band is generous (48–64px top and bottom), making this section occupy roughly 1/3 to 1/2 of a viewport height and helping it stand out as a key reassurance area before the footer content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-width band; it scrolls like other sections but visually breaks out of the central column via its background while keeping text and interactive elements aligned to the main grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cross-page-links-block is the final primary content element of the About page, placed directly below the technology-safety-section with approximately 48–56px of vertical separation so it feels like a concluding call-to-action cluster. It returns to a plain white background that matches the page’s body, with all content centered within the main ~1140–1200px container. The block’s height is modest—roughly 1/4 of the viewport on desktop—with vertical padding of about 32–40px. Inside, a concise heading or prompt sits near the top (e.g., ‘Learn More’), followed by 2–3 horizontally arranged link elements such as buttons or small cards that point to related pages (e.g., ‘Meet Our Dentists’, ‘New Patient Information’). These links are centered horizontally within the column, each taking about 20–25% of the content width with 16–24px gaps between them, preserving substantial whitespace around the cluster. The links use brand teal/green styling and subtle shadows to echo the primary CTAs seen in the header while maintaining a secondary level of emphasis at the end of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static footer-adjacent block; scrolls naturally and visually transitions into the site-wide footer below without sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-error-display is an inline status indicator positioned near the top of the About content column so that any issues with loading practice information are immediately noticeable. When active, it appears directly below the patient-focus toggle and just above the mission-section, spanning the full width of the central container (~1140–1200px). In its error state, it takes the form of a horizontally centered alert bar or card with 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding, using a contrasting but brand-appropriate color (e.g., soft red or amber) and subtle shadow, pushing the rest of the content (mission and below) downward. Its height is modest—typically not more than 1/8 of the viewport—but it commands attention due to its color contrast. In a loading state, it can show a centered spinner and message within the same full-width area, keeping overall vertical usage compact while clearly signaling the state above the scrollable content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears inline within the content flow; does not overlay other sections. When present, it shifts subsequent content downward. It scrolls with the page like any other block-level element.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: This page dynamically loads and displays the practice’s privacy policy, including how information from appointment requests, contact forms, and emergency messages is handled, using the legal content API.

core_features: Fetch and display the latest Privacy Policy content using the getLegalPageContent interface based on a URL parameter-driven pageType.
Show the policy title and last updated date returned from the legal content API.
Render the full policy body as HTML, including any sections describing handling of appointment requests, contact submissions, and emergency messages.
Support deep-linking to specific policy sections via URL parameters so users can be directed to relevant parts of the policy.
Provide basic actions such as printing the policy and copying a direct link to the current section without storing any data locally.
Handle loading and error states for the legal content fetch, with a retry mechanism if the API call fails.
user_workflows: Accessing the Privacy Policy from footer navigation: (1) User clicks the "Privacy Policy" link in the site footer, which opens privacy.html (optionally with a pageType query parameter). (2) On page load, the legal-page-loader component reads the pageType query parameter (or falls back to a default for the privacy policy) and calls getLegalPageContent with that value. (3) While the request is in progress, the legal-loading-indicator component shows a loading state. (4) When the API response arrives, the legal-content-viewer renders the title, last_updated date, and content_html from getLegalPageContent. (5) The user reads the policy, scrolls, and optionally uses the toolbar to print or navigate.

Navigating directly to a specific policy section (e.g., from a form): (1) User clicks a contextual "Privacy Policy" link next to an appointment, emergency, or contact form, which navigates to privacy.html with a section query parameter (for example, privacy.html?pageType=<privacyPageType>&section=<sectionKey>). (2) The legal-page-loader calls getLegalPageContent using pageType from the URL. (3) After content_html is rendered, the legal-content-viewer checks the section query parameter and attempts to scroll to or focus the matching anchor or element within the policy content. (4) The user can adjust the view, copy a link to the current section, or remove the section parameter via navigation controls if they want to see the entire policy from the top.

Printing or saving the Privacy Policy: (1) User opens privacy.html and lets the legal-page-loader fetch the policy via getLegalPageContent. (2) Once the policy is visible, the user clicks a "Print" action in the legal-page-toolbar. (3) The toolbar triggers the browser’s print dialog, optionally adding or updating a mode=print query parameter to the URL so that layout or navigation visibility can be adjusted for printing. (4) The user completes printing or saves the page as a PDF using the browser’s native print capabilities.
interactions: Page load: triggers getLegalPageContent with a pageType value derived from the URL query parameters; if none is provided, a default pageType for the privacy policy is used.
Scroll interaction: users scroll through the rendered content_html; the viewer does not modify the content, only displays it as provided by the API.

Section navigation: when a URL includes a section query parameter, the page attempts to scroll to a matching element in content_html after it has been rendered; if the user clicks a different in-page link, the section parameter in the URL can be updated to reflect the new position.
Print action: user clicks a toolbar button to invoke the browser’s print dialog; the component may update the URL to include mode=print and suppress non-essential UI while printing.
Retry after error: if getLegalPageContent fails, an error message and "Try again" button are shown; the user can click the button to re-trigger the API call without reloading the entire page.

Copy link action: user clicks a button in the toolbar to copy a direct link to the current page state (including pageType and section query parameters) to their clipboard, allowing easy sharing or bookmarking of a specific part of the policy.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies solely on URL query parameters to control its behavior and does not use localStorage or other client-side persistence. The primary parameters are: (1) pageType: determines which legal content is loaded; on initial load, the legal-page-loader reads privacy.html?pageType=<value> and passes that value into getLegalPageContent(pageType). If pageType is absent, the component uses a predefined default that corresponds to the Privacy Policy. (2) section: an optional identifier for a specific section of the policy; after getLegalPageContent returns and content_html is rendered, the legal-content-viewer attempts to locate an element corresponding to the section value (for example, by matching an id or data attribute within the rendered HTML) and scrolls the viewport to that element. If no match is found, the page remains at the top. When the user navigates to a different section via in-page links, the component can update the section parameter in the URL using history APIs without reloading the page. (3) mode: an optional rendering mode (e.g., mode=print) that influences layout behavior; when mode=print is present, the page may hide non-essential UI components while still fetching content via getLegalPageContent in the same way. Toggling mode updates the URL and triggers a re-render of the layout but does not change the underlying legal data. All state transitions (changing pageType, section, or mode) are handled through URL parameter updates and subsequent re-rendering, ensuring that sharing or bookmarking the URL preserves the exact policy view and behavior.

components.functionality: Reads the pageType query parameter from the URL, calls getLegalPageContent with that value (or a default for the Privacy Policy if absent), manages loading and error state, and exposes the resulting title, last_updated, and content_html to child components.

Receives the title, last_updated, and content_html from the legal-page-loader and renders them on the page. After rendering content_html, it interprets the section query parameter (if present) to scroll to or highlight the corresponding portion of the policy. It also listens to changes in URL parameters to adjust the scroll position when section changes.

Provides user actions related to the Privacy Policy view, such as invoking browser print, navigating back using browser history, scrolling to the top of the policy, and copying the current URL (including pageType and section parameters) to the clipboard for sharing or bookmarking.
Displays a loading state while the getLegalPageContent call is in progress and hides itself when the legal content is successfully loaded or an error occurs.

Shows an error message and retry control if getLegalPageContent fails, without exposing any static fallback policy text. When the user clicks retry, it delegates to the legal-page-loader to call getLegalPageContent again using the current pageType URL parameter.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a max-width content container (~1140–1200px). The row of four circular service cards suggests four equal-width columns on desktop.
layout.layout_pattern: All legal components sit within the main central content column that begins directly below the existing hero band and row of four circular service cards. The flow remains strictly top-to-bottom: hero + circles (existing), then the Privacy Policy heading and introductory text (rendered through the legal-content-viewer), followed immediately by the legal-page-toolbar aligned within that same column, and then the long-form policy text. The legal-page-loader is invisible, orchestrating which of the three visible states occupies the main content area: legal-loading-indicator, legal-error-state, or legal-content-viewer.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All legal components sit within the main central content column that begins directly below the existing hero band and row of four circular service cards. The flow remains strictly top-to-bottom: hero + circles (existing), then the Privacy Policy heading and introductory text (rendered through the legal-content-viewer), followed immediately by the legal-page-toolbar aligned within that same column, and then the long-form policy text. The legal-page-loader is invisible, orchestrating which of the three visible states occupies the main content area: legal-loading-indicator, legal-error-state, or legal-content-viewer.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The privacy policy area uses the same 12-column center-aligned grid as the rest of the site. On desktop, the legal content region occupies roughly 60–70% of the total page width (about 720–840px within a ~1140–1200px container), centered with generous left/right margins. Vertically, the header + nav + hero + circular cards continue to take up the upper 40–50% of the initial viewport. The Privacy Policy heading block and toolbar together fill another ~15–20% of the viewport height, with the remainder devoted to the scrolling policy body. The loading and error states each sit in the same vertical band reserved for the policy content, taking up about 50–60% of the initial viewport height so they feel substantial but not overwhelming.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density matches an editorial legal page: generous line spacing and margins, not wall-of-text. Headings and intro text are spaced with ~32–48px vertical gaps from surrounding elements, while paragraphs have consistent 16–24px spacing. The toolbar cluster is compact but not cramped, with ~12–16px padding inside buttons and ~8–16px gaps between them. Loading and error messages are concise, centered within a card-like area with ample whitespace, mirroring the moderate, breathable feel of the hero and circular cards rather than cramming content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye follows the original Z-pattern: logo to top-right CTAs, down to the center row of circular cards. From there it drops further down into the Privacy Policy area, where a large centered heading (within the legal-content-viewer) anchors attention. The gaze then shifts slightly right to the toolbar actions, and finally settles into a top-to-bottom reading flow through the policy text. In loading or error states, the eye lands centrally on the message and indicator, framed by the same central column and whitespace, clearly signaling the current state before content appears or is retried.

layout.overall_description: Below the established header, navigation bar, and full-width row of four circular service cards, the page transitions into a quieter, editorial-style Privacy Policy section that sits in the center of the screen. A centered content column, about two-thirds the width of the overall page, begins with a large, serif-styled 'Privacy Policy' heading and a brief, centered welcome or explanatory line rendered by the legal-content-viewer. Directly aligned beneath this heading block, and flush with the right edge of the same column, sits a slim horizontal toolbar for legal actions (such as printing or downloading), while maintaining generous white space above and below so the area still feels open and non-technical. The main legal text is left-aligned within this central column, scrolling downward with consistent, comfortable spacing and typographic hierarchy. When the privacy policy is loading, the usual content area is temporarily replaced by a centered loading indicator card within this column, vertically positioned so it appears immediately below the hero band, occupying roughly half the viewport height. If an error occurs, a similarly placed but slightly more prominent error card with an icon and retry action appears in that same footprint, keeping all states visually anchored to the same central, trustworthy reading area that echoes the layout rhythm and spacing of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The legal-page-loader functions as a behind-the-scenes wrapper around the entire privacy policy content region within the main central column. It does not have a visible presence or occupy explicit space in the layout, but conceptually spans the full width of the central content column (about 60–70% of the page width) and the full height of the policy content area, from just below the circular service cards and H1 intro block down to the end of the legal text. Its role is to determine which visual child component is shown in that reserved content footprint: while data is being fetched, it swaps in the legal-loading-indicator; on success, it reveals the legal-content-viewer and the legal-page-toolbar; on failure, it replaces the content with the legal-error-state. This maintains a stable spatial region for the policy content, so the user always looks to the same central area of the page for updates, mirroring the predictable, stacked content behavior seen elsewhere in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller that conceptually wraps the privacy policy content region; it does not affect layout directly but toggles visibility of child components that occupy the central content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The legal-content-viewer is the primary content block in the Privacy Policy area and sits within the central content column directly beneath the circular service cards band and the existing H1/subheading spacing. It is horizontally centered on the page, occupying approximately 60–70% of the overall page width, aligned to the same max-width container as the rest of the site. The top of this component begins about 48px below the bottom edge of the circular cards band, preserving the generous section separation seen in the mockup. At its top, the viewer renders a large, centered serif heading (e.g., 'Privacy Policy') spanning the full width of the column, with roughly 24–32px of space above and below. Directly under that, a shorter, centered welcome or explanatory line appears in sans-serif text with slightly smaller size and lighter weight, maintaining about 16–24px of vertical gap to the body content region below. The main legal text then flows downward as left-aligned copy within the same column, creating a subtle shift from the centered heading to a more traditional reading alignment. Paragraphs and subheadings stack with 16–24px vertical spacing, and section headings within the policy use a bit more breathing room (around 24–32px above) to segment content. The component itself extends vertically as long as needed to accommodate the entire API-loaded policy, effectively occupying the majority of the scrolling space beneath the hero for this page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level scrolling content within the central column; it expands vertically with the length of the legal text and pushes the page footer downward without fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The legal-page-toolbar is positioned within the same central content column as the legal-content-viewer, visually attached to the top of the policy content but slightly separated from the main heading. It appears just below the subheading or introductory sentence that the content viewer renders, with about 16–24px of vertical space above it to avoid crowding the title. Horizontally, the toolbar forms a short horizontal strip that aligns its right edge to the right edge of the content column, echoing the right-aligned CTA cluster in the header. Its width is dictated by its contents (e.g., 2–4 buttons or links), typically spanning about 25–40% of the central column, leaving ample empty space to its left. The toolbar height is modest (roughly 40–48px), with individual action buttons styled as pill or rounded rectangles with around 12–16px of horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding, and 8–16px gaps between actions. There is approximately 24–32px of vertical space between the toolbar and the first block of body text below it, ensuring the page maintains the airy, editorial feel rather than appearing like a dense control panel. As the user scrolls, the toolbar moves along with the content; it does not float or stick, but is easily rediscoverable by scrolling back to the top of the privacy policy content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, non-sticky horizontal toolbar anchored near the top of the central content column, right-aligned within that column and scrolling naturally with the policy content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The legal-loading-indicator occupies the exact content footprint reserved for the privacy policy text within the central column whenever the policy is being fetched. Horizontally, it is centered and constrained to the same 60–70% page-width content column, matching the alignment of the legal-content-viewer. Vertically, when first shown, it begins roughly 48px below the circular cards band, so it appears immediately where a user’s eye expects the Privacy Policy heading to be. The indicator is presented as a visually soft, card-like area with a subtle drop shadow, spanning about 60–80% of the column’s width and centered within it, leaving uniform margins on the left and right. Inside this card, a circular or ring-style loading spinner, styled in the brand teal/green, is centered both horizontally and vertically, with a brief 'Loading Privacy Policy…' message set in sans-serif text directly beneath it. The combined spinner and text area occupies roughly the middle third of the card height, with generous padding of about 24–32px on all sides. The overall loading block takes up approximately half to two-thirds of the viewport height, ensuring it is the dominant element in the content area but still surrounded by open whitespace above and below, consistent with the site’s moderate, open density. Once loading completes, this entire indicator smoothly gives way to the legal-content-viewer and toolbar in the same spatial region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the main policy content region within the central column; centered within that region and scrolls with the page if additional content above pushes it downward, but typically appears near the top of the viewport immediately after the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The legal-error-state appears in place of the main policy content whenever there is a problem loading the privacy policy. It is positioned identically to the loading indicator and content viewer: within the central content column, centered horizontally, beginning about 48px below the circular cards band. The error display takes the form of a slightly more prominent card with a soft shadow and a subtle colored border or accent in a warning hue that complements the brand palette without feeling alarming. The card spans roughly 60–80% of the content column’s width and is centered, leaving symmetrical side margins. At the top-left inside the card, a medium-sized circular icon (e.g., an exclamation mark) in brand teal or a muted warning color anchors attention, echoing the circular motif of the hero cards but on a smaller scale. To the right of or just below this icon, a clear, concise error headline (e.g., 'We’re having trouble loading our Privacy Policy') is displayed in a slightly larger sans-serif weight, followed by a short explanatory line of body text. Below the message, and right-aligned within the card, a primary action button (such as 'Retry') is provided, with 12–16px padding and following the same visual style as the site’s other CTAs, but scaled appropriately for this context. The vertical padding inside the card is generous (around 24–32px), and the card overall occupies about 40–60% of the initial viewport height, leaving open space above and below to maintain the page’s calm, trustworthy feel. When the user retries successfully, this error block is replaced by the loading indicator and then by the normal legal-content-viewer, all within the same central spatial area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as a block-level card that replaces the policy content region within the central column; it scrolls naturally with the page and does not use fixed or overlay behavior, ensuring the error feels integrated into the normal reading flow.

name: Terms of Use
title: Terms of Use
description: Page that dynamically loads and displays the practice’s Terms of Use and related legal information for website usage and online tools.

core_features: Load Terms of Use content from the legal content API using the appropriate pageType.
Render the full legal HTML content returned by the API, including medical-information disclaimers and online booking limitations.
Display the last-updated date for the Terms of Use, as returned by the API.
Support deep-linking to specific sections of the Terms via URL parameters and/or hash fragments.
Provide an in-page table of contents (TOC) generated from headings in the legal HTML content to help users navigate long terms.
Allow users to print the Terms of Use via a dedicated print action.
Handle loading and error states if the legal content API call is pending or fails.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View overall Terms of Use
1. User clicks the "Terms of Use" link in the footer and lands on terms.html.
2. The page controller determines the legal pageType from configuration or DOM metadata associated with this page.
3. The legal-content-loader component calls getLegalPageContent(pageType).
4. While waiting, a loading state is shown.
5. On success, the legal-content-viewer renders content_html and the last-updated-indicator shows last_updated.
6. User scrolls and reads the Terms of Use, including sections on acceptable use, medical disclaimers, and online booking tools.

Workflow: Deep-link to a specific Terms section (e.g., from booking flow)
1. User clicks a link from another page (for example, the booking or emergency information page) that sends them to terms.html with a query parameter and/or hash, such as terms.html?section={sectionId} or terms.html#{sectionId}.
2. The terms-page-controller reads the URL parameters and hash on load.
3. The legal-content-loader fetches the Terms via getLegalPageContent(pageType).
4. After content_html is rendered, the legal-section-navigator locates the element whose id matches the section value and scrolls to it.
5. The TOC (if present) highlights the active section so the user understands where they are in the document.

Workflow: Navigate within Terms using table of contents
1. User opens terms.html, content is loaded via getLegalPageContent.
2. The legal-toc-generator parses the rendered legal content (e.g., H2/H3 headings) to build a structured TOC.
3. The TOC is displayed as a list of clickable section items.
4. User clicks a TOC entry; legal-section-navigator updates the URL (e.g., ?section={id} and/or hash #{id}) and scrolls smoothly to the associated heading in content_html.
5. If the user uses the browser back/forward buttons, the controller listens to URL changes and re-syncs the scroll position and TOC highlight to the current section parameter/hash.

Workflow: Print the Terms of Use
1. User opens terms.html and waits for the Terms content to load.
2. User clicks a "Print" control on the page.
3. The legal-print-control triggers the browser’s print dialog (e.g., window.print()).
4. The user confirms printing in the browser UI and obtains a hard copy of the Terms.

Workflow: Handle API error when loading Terms
1. User navigates to terms.html.
2. The legal-content-loader calls getLegalPageContent(pageType).
3. If the request fails (network/server error), the loader sets the page state to "error" and passes error details to the legal-error-banner.
4. The legal-error-banner displays a non-technical message indicating the Terms cannot be loaded right now and offers a retry action.
5. User clicks retry; the loader calls getLegalPageContent(pageType) again.
6. On success, the error banner hides and the Terms content is displayed.
interactions: On initial page load, the terms-page-controller reads URL query parameters (e.g., pageType override, section) and hash fragment, then initializes the legal-content-loader.
The legal-content-loader calls getLegalPageContent with a pageType derived from page-level configuration or URL parameters and listens for the asynchronous response.
The legal-content-viewer injects content_html into the document and attaches delegated click listeners to internal anchor links so that section changes can update URL parameters for consistency.
The legal-toc-navigation component allows users to click section titles; clicks trigger scrolling to the target section and update the section query parameter and/or hash.
The last-updated-indicator binds to last_updated from getLegalPageContent and displays it; no user interaction is required, but the date updates whenever content is re-fetched.
The legal-print-control exposes a clickable control; on click, it calls the browser’s print mechanism and may temporarily adjust the layout for print if needed.
If getLegalPageContent fails, the legal-error-banner appears with a retry button; clicking retry triggers the loader to attempt the API call again.

When users use browser back/forward navigation that changes URL parameters or hash, the terms-page-controller listens to those events and updates the visible section highlighting and scroll position accordingly without reloading the entire page unless the pageType parameter itself changed.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Terms of Use page state is controlled entirely via URL and in-memory state, without localStorage or other persistent clients.

1. pageType parameter:
- Query parameter: terms.html?pageType={value}
- If present, this value is passed directly to getLegalPageContent(pageType).
- If absent, the controller derives a default pageType from a non-hardcoded, page-level configuration (for example, a data attribute on the root element or server-rendered metadata associated with this specific file).
- If pageType changes (via navigation or manual URL edit), the page re-calls getLegalPageContent with the new value and fully replaces the displayed content_html and last_updated.

2. section parameter and fragment identifier:
- Query parameter: terms.html?section={sectionId}
- Hash fragment: terms.html#{sectionId}
- On load and on URL change (popstate/hashchange), the controller determines the target sectionId using a simple precedence rule (for example, query parameter over hash, or hash if query parameter is absent).
- After content_html is rendered, the legal-section-navigator searches for an element whose id matches sectionId. If found, the page scrolls to it and marks it as active (for TOC highlighting).
- When a user clicks a TOC item or an internal anchor within content_html, the controller updates the section parameter and/or hash in the URL using history.pushState or history.replaceState, ensuring that the URL always reflects the current section for deep-linking and back/forward navigation.

3. Language or variant (optional):
- An optional lang parameter (e.g., terms.html?lang={code}) can exist; the page combines this with the base pageType to construct the pageType value that is passed into getLegalPageContent, relying on backend support for localized or variant legal text.
- If lang changes in the URL while on the page, the controller recomputes pageType accordingly, re-fetches content via getLegalPageContent, and re-renders the content_html and last_updated.

4. Transient UI state:
- Loading and error states are maintained only in memory within the components (e.g., isLoading, hasError) and are reset whenever getLegalPageContent is called.
- No terms content is stored in localStorage or other persistent client storage; the source of truth remains the getLegalPageContent response for the current pageType.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL state, triggers loading of legal content for the Terms of Use, manages section navigation and orchestrates child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Read URL parameters and hash, derive effective pageType and sectionId, then request legal content via legal-content-loader.
onUrlChange: Listen to popstate and hashchange; if pageType changed, re-trigger getLegalPageContent, otherwise update active section highlighting and scroll.
onContentLoaded: After legal-content-viewer finishes rendering content_html, notify legal-section-navigator and legal-toc-navigation so they can align with the current sectionId.
onSectionChange: Receive sectionId from TOC or internal link clicks, update URL query/hash using history APIs, and instruct legal-section-navigator to scroll to the corresponding element.
onRetryLoad: When triggered by the error banner, instruct the legal-content-loader to re-call getLegalPageContent using the current pageType.

components.functionality: Calls getLegalPageContent with the correct pageType and exposes loading, success, and error states to other components.
event_handlers: onInitLoad: Invoked by terms-page-controller with a resolved pageType; calls getLegalPageContent(pageType) and sets isLoading=true.
onLoadSuccess: When the API returns successfully, sets isLoading=false, hasError=false, caches the response in memory, and notifies terms-page-controller and legal-content-viewer.
onLoadError: When the API call fails, sets isLoading=false, hasError=true, stores error details, and notifies legal-error-banner.
onRetry: When requested by the controller or error banner, re-calls getLegalPageContent using the last requested pageType.

components.functionality: Renders the Terms of Use HTML returned by getLegalPageContent and enables internal link handling while remaining agnostic of the legal text contents.
event_handlers: onRender: When new content_html is provided, clears any previous content, injects the new HTML into the content region, and triggers a content-rendered event for other components.

onInternalLinkClick: Delegated click handler for anchor tags within content_html; if an anchor targets an in-page id or hash, prevent full page reload, update URL section/hash via the controller, and trigger section-change events.
onScroll: Optional listener that can report the currently visible heading back to the terms-page-controller so that the active TOC entry and URL section parameter can be updated.

components.functionality: Displays the last-updated date for the Terms of Use and updates automatically whenever the legal content is re-fetched.
event_handlers: onLastUpdatedChange: When last_updated changes from the loader, update the displayed date string.
onVisibilityToggle: Optionally hide the indicator if last_updated is absent or invalid, based on the API response.

components.functionality: Builds a table of contents from headings in the rendered legal content and lets users jump to specific sections of the Terms of Use.
event_handlers: onContentRendered: After legal-content-viewer renders content_html, scan the DOM for heading elements (e.g., specific levels or data attributes) to build a structured TOC model in memory.
onTocItemClick: When a user clicks a TOC entry, emit a section-change event with the target heading’s id to the terms-page-controller, which updates URL parameters and triggers scrolling.
onSectionStateChange: When URL section/hash changes or scroll events indicate a new active section, highlight the corresponding TOC entry.

components.functionality: Controls scrolling to and focusing specific sections of the Terms content based on URL state or user actions.
event_handlers: onSectionTargetResolved: When terms-page-controller resolves a sectionId (from URL or TOC click) and content is available, scrolls to the DOM element with the matching id and optionally applies a temporary highlight.
onContentReloaded: When a new content_html is rendered (e.g., due to pageType change), re-apply current sectionId to ensure the user stays on the contextually relevant section.
onNoMatchingSection: If no element matches the requested sectionId, optionally scroll to the top of the content and clear active section highlighting.

components.functionality: Provides a control users can click to print the currently displayed Terms of Use content.
event_handlers: onClickPrint: When the user clicks the control, trigger the browser’s print dialog for the current page.
